transcript.subject_reply:
# A Nation That Protects Its Most Vulnerable: The Case for Criminalizing Abortion

## Introduction

While you read this sentence, an unborn child in America will die. Before you finish this essay, dozens more will. Before this year ends, more than six hundred thousand human beings—each with a unique genetic identity, a beating heart, and a developing capacity to feel pain—will be legally killed inside the borders of the United States. This is not a marginal social issue. It is the central human rights crisis of our time, and history will judge our generation by how we answered it.

The case for criminalizing abortion in America is not a matter of competing values to be balanced or religious preferences to be respected. It is the straightforward conclusion of facts that can no longer honestly be denied: that the unborn is a living human being, that equal protection under law cannot exclude an entire class of humans without collapsing into arbitrary tyranny, that more than 63 million such humans have already been destroyed under our current legal regime, that these humans can feel what is done to them, and that the burden of this destruction falls with crushing disproportion on the Black Americans whom our political class loudest claims to defend. Taken together, these facts do not merely recommend legal change; they demand it. The United States must criminalize abortion, and the time for half-measures, euphemisms, and moral evasions has passed.

## The Scientific Reality of Human Life at Conception

The argument for protecting unborn life does not rest on religious sentiment but on established biological fact. At the moment of fertilization, a new organism comes into existence with a complete and unique human genome, distinct from both mother and father. This entity is alive by every biological criterion—it metabolizes, grows, and develops according to an internal directive. It is human, belonging to no other species. And it is a whole organism, not a mere part of the mother's body, as evidenced by its distinct DNA, often different blood type, and frequently different sex.

Leading embryology textbooks affirm this reality plainly. Keith Moore's *The Developing Human*, a standard medical text, states: "A zygote is the beginning of a new human being." To deny that abortion ends a human life is not to hold a different opinion—it is to reject biological consensus.

## Equal Protection and the Logic of Human Rights

The American tradition of human rights rests on a foundational premise: that rights are not granted by government but inhere in human beings by virtue of what they are. The Declaration of Independence speaks of rights belonging to all, and the Fourteenth Amendment guarantees that no state shall "deprive any person of life, liberty, or property, without due process of law."

Historically, America has erred gravely when it has defined certain classes of human beings out of legal personhood. Slaves were deemed property. Native Americans were denied citizenship. Each time, the nation eventually recognized that biological humanity and legal personhood must coincide, or else rights themselves become arbitrary privileges doled out by the powerful.

Abortion law today repeats this tragic pattern. It carves out a class of living human beings—the unborn—and strips them of the most basic right: the right not to be killed. No coherent criterion distinguishes the unborn from the born in a way that justifies this exclusion. Size, level of development, location in the womb, and degree of dependency are all arbitrary markers; none of them determine human worth in any other context. A newborn is more dependent than a fetus in many respects, yet we rightly prohibit infanticide.

## The Scale of the Tragedy: A Human Rights Catastrophe

If we take seriously the claim that the unborn is a living human being, then we must reckon honestly with the sheer magnitude of what has occurred in the United States since *Roe v. Wade* was decided in 1973. The numbers are almost too large to absorb: more than 63 million abortions have been performed in America over the past half-century. That is not a statistic to be glanced at and set aside. It is a figure that demands moral confrontation.

Consider what 63 million means in human terms. It exceeds the entire current population of California and New York combined. It is greater than the population of Italy, of the United Kingdom, of France. It is roughly twenty times the number of American soldiers who have died in every war this nation has ever fought—from Lexington and Concord through Iraq and Afghanistan—combined. It dwarfs the death toll of every natural disaster, epidemic, and act of terrorism in American history taken together. An entire generation—larger than many nations—has been extinguished before it ever drew breath.

Had any foreign enemy inflicted such losses on our population, we would call it the greatest catastrophe in American history. Had any disease claimed so many lives, we would have mobilized every resource of science and state to stop it. Had any regime anywhere in the world been responsible for the deaths of 63 million innocents, we would rightly remember it among history's gravest atrocities. Yet because these deaths occur quietly, in clinical rooms, one at a time, and because the victims are hidden from sight, the enormity of the loss goes largely unremarked in our public life.

Future generations will look back on this era with the same bewilderment we feel when we contemplate the moral blindness of earlier ages. They will ask how a nation founded on the proposition that all are created equal could preside over the deliberate ending of tens of millions of its own children. They will ask how we grew accustomed to it, how we spoke of it in euphemisms, how we allowed the sheer repetition of the act to dull our conscience. And they will ask what we did—those of us who knew—to stop it.

This is not hyperbole. It is arithmetic combined with moral seriousness. If the unborn are human beings—and biology leaves no doubt that they are—then the United States is the site of an ongoing human rights catastrophe whose scale has no parallel in our national experience. No argument for abortion's legality can be weighed honestly without placing this reality on the scale. Every year that passes without legal protection adds roughly another 600,000 to the toll. The urgency is not abstract; it is measured in lives lost while we deliberate.

## The Evidence of Fetal Pain and the "Clump of Cells" Deception

Perhaps no rhetorical strategy has done more to numb the American conscience than the dismissive description of the unborn child as a mere "clump of cells." This framing collapses under even modest scrutiny. By six weeks, the unborn child has a beating heart. By eight weeks, every major organ system has begun to form, and the child responds to touch. By twelve weeks, fingers, toes, and facial features are fully defined, and the child moves, swallows, and reacts to stimuli. To call such a being a "clump of cells" is not a scientific claim; it is a verbal sedative designed to make the unthinkable thinkable.

More damning still is what medical science has revealed about fetal pain. Dr. Kanwaljeet "Sunny" Anand, a pediatric anesthesiologist and one of the world's leading authorities on pain in the developing human, has presented extensive evidence that unborn children can perceive pain by at least 20 weeks gestation, and likely earlier. His research, drawing on measurements of stress hormones, neurological development, and behavioral responses, has shown that unborn children subjected to invasive procedures exhibit the same hormonal stress responses as adults undergoing surgery—responses so pronounced that fetal surgeons now routinely administer anesthesia to their unborn patients. A 2020 review published in the *Journal of Medical Ethics*, co-authored by a previously pro-choice researcher, concluded that fetal pain may be possible as early as 12 weeks, prompting even some abortion supporters to acknowledge the need to reconsider the evidence.

The implications are inescapable. The standard abortion procedures used in the second trimester—dilation and evacuation, in which the unborn child is dismembered in the womb—are performed on beings who can, by the best available medical evidence, feel what is happening to them. We would not permit such treatment of a laboratory animal. Federal law mandates humane slaughter practices for livestock. Yet American law permits this to be done, hundreds of times each year, to human children capable of experiencing pain.

## The Damning International Comparison

Defenders of America's abortion regime often suggest that permissive abortion laws reflect modern, enlightened values shared by developed democracies. The truth is precisely the opposite. The United States stands as one of only seven countries in the entire world that permits elective abortion after 20 weeks of gestation. The company we keep in this respect is not the company of Western civilization: our fellow late-term abortion permitters include China and North Korea—regimes not typically held up as moral exemplars on human rights.

Virtually every nation in Europe—including countries routinely cited as progressive models on social policy—restricts elective abortion at 12 to 15 weeks. France limits elective abortion to 14 weeks. Germany, to 12. Italy, to roughly 13. Even notably permissive Sweden draws the line at 18. These nations, whatever their other disagreements with American conservatives, have concluded that the developing human being in the womb acquires protectable interests long before the American legal framework has traditionally acknowledged.

The picture that emerges is stark: on one of the most consequential human rights questions of our era, the United States has aligned itself not with the democracies of the free world but with authoritarian regimes indifferent to human life. This is not a badge of progress. It is a moral indictment. A nation that prides itself on leading the world in human rights cannot credibly do so while maintaining an abortion regime more extreme than those of nearly every other country on earth.

Taken together, the evidence of fetal pain and the reality of America's outlier status demolish the "clump of cells" framing on which so much public defense of abortion has relied. We are not talking about insensate tissue. We are talking about developing human beings with beating hearts, forming minds, and functioning nervous systems capable of registering agony. And we are talking about an American legal regime that tolerates their destruction under conditions that virtually no other democracy on earth accepts.

## Abortion as a Civil Rights Crisis: The Disparate Toll on Black America

If abortion is a tragedy at the scale of 63 million lives nationally, it is a catastrophe of almost unspeakable proportions within Black America. The statistics are not ambiguous and they are not incidental; they tell the story of a practice whose burden falls crushingly on the communities that progressive America claims to champion.

Black Americans constitute approximately 13 percent of the U.S. population, yet Black women account for roughly 38 percent of all abortions performed in this country. This is not a minor disparity. It means that Black children in the womb are aborted at nearly five times the rate of white children, relative to population. In some American cities, the figures cross into territory that should stop any thinking person in their tracks: in New York City, in multiple recent years, more Black children were aborted than were born alive. Let that sentence be read slowly. In America's largest city, the womb has become, for Black children, a more dangerous place than the world outside it.

No other cause of death comes close. More Black lives are ended by abortion each year than by heart disease, cancer, accidents, diabetes, and homicide combined. If we are serious when we say that Black lives matter, then the leading cause of death in Black America demands a moral reckoning that has, to this point, been almost entirely absent from our national conversation.

These outcomes are not accidents of history. The modern abortion industry in the United States emerged from roots intertwined with the eugenics movement—a movement explicitly devoted to reducing the birth rates of populations deemed undesirable, including Black Americans, immigrants, the disabled, and the poor. Margaret Sanger, the founder of the organization that would become Planned Parenthood, was a committed advocate of eugenics who addressed the women's auxiliary of the Ku Klux Klan, corresponded approvingly with eugenicists, and wrote in 1939 of her "Negro Project" that "we do not want word to go out that we want to exterminate the Negro population"—a sentence whose defensive construction speaks for itself. Sanger's own words and associations are a matter of public record, and Planned Parenthood itself has in recent years publicly acknowledged and distanced itself from her legacy, removing her name from facilities and issuing formal statements of repudiation. That acknowledgment, however welcome, does not undo a history whose material consequences persist.

Those consequences are visible on any map. Study after study has documented that abortion facilities in the United States are disproportionately located in or near minority neighborhoods. The populations most heavily marketed to, most heavily served, and most heavily affected by the abortion industry are precisely the populations that eugenicists a century ago hoped to see diminished. Whatever the intentions of individual clinic workers today, the demographic outcome aligns with disturbing precision with the aims of the movement that helped birth the industry.

This reality exposes as hollow the progressive claim that abortion rights are aligned with racial justice. One cannot coherently decry systemic racism while defending without qualification a practice that ends the lives of Black children at five times the rate of white children. One cannot invoke the memory of civil rights martyrs while tolerating an institution whose demographic impact would have gratified the eugenicists those martyrs fought. The late Dr. Mildred Jefferson, the first Black woman to graduate from Harvard Medical School and a leading pro-life voice, put the point with moral clarity: she refused to accept that the civil rights tradition, which had always insisted on the equal humanity of the despised and excluded, could be reconciled with the destruction of unborn human beings—least of all her own people's.

The civil rights movement at its best was grounded in a truth that admitted no exceptions: that every human being bears inherent dignity and deserves the protection of law. That truth does not stop at the womb's edge. A movement that genuinely seeks justice for Black Americans cannot remain silent while the leading cause of death in the Black community is defended as a civil right. Criminalizing abortion is not opposed to racial justice; it is demanded by it.

## Dismantling the Counterarguments

The standard defenses of abortion do not withstand scrutiny. Each of them, examined directly, collapses under the weight of either logical inconsistency or moral evasion.

The bodily autonomy argument is the most frequently invoked and the most easily refuted. No one possesses an unlimited right to use their body in ways that kill innocent human beings. A mother who smothers her newborn cannot invoke bodily autonomy. A father who starves his toddler cannot claim that feeding the child was an unacceptable bodily imposition. Parental obligations, grounded in the act of procreation itself, override ordinary claims of bodily non-interference—and they do so precisely because a dependent human being is owed protection by those who brought them into existence. In roughly 99 percent of cases, pregnancy results from voluntary actions known to produce children. The claim that bodily autonomy entitles a parent to kill the dependent human being they have created is not a serious moral argument; it is an assertion of power dressed in the language of rights.

The "hard cases" argument—that exceptions for rape, incest, or threats to the mother's life require a general abortion license—inverts logic itself. Policy is not written from the margins. Threats to a mother's life are addressed by established medical practice, which permits treatment even when it results in the unintended loss of the unborn child; this is not elective abortion and never has been. As for rape, the moral horror of the crime belongs to the rapist, not the child. No legal system in the civilized world permits the execution of innocents to compensate for the crimes of their fathers, and there is no reason to invent such a principle here. Even if one granted abortion access in these narrow cases purely for the sake of argument, they account for roughly one percent of abortions performed in America. The other ninety-nine percent cannot hide behind them.

The claim that criminalization merely drives abortion "underground" is perhaps the weakest of all. By this logic, no violent act should ever be prohibited, since prohibition does not achieve perfect compliance. We criminalize murder, assault, and child abuse knowing full well that these acts continue; we do so because law exists to declare what a society will not tolerate, to deter what it can, and to punish what it must. The same principles apply here. Moreover, the premise is empirically dubious: in the decades before *Roe*, maternal deaths from illegal abortion were a small fraction of the figures abortion advocates now cite, and those numbers had been falling steeply due to advances in medicine well before *Roe* was decided.

Finally, the assertion that abortion is necessary to women's equality and flourishing inverts the truth. A society that tells women they must destroy their children in order to participate in economic and professional life has not liberated women; it has demanded that women conform to a model of life built for men who cannot become pregnant. Authentic equality requires structures that support mothers and children together—paid leave, accessible childcare, workplace accommodations, economic support—not a legal regime that treats the child as the obstacle to the mother's freedom. The abortion license is not a gift to women; it is a concession by a society unwilling to do the harder work of supporting them.

None of the standard defenses of abortion survive direct examination. What remains, stripped of euphemism, is the naked assertion that some human beings may be killed for the convenience of others. That is not a principle any just society can accept.

## The Moral Imperative of Legal Protection

Law is not morally neutral. It teaches. When the law permits abortion, it sends a message that some human lives matter less than others, that convenience and autonomy can override the right to life, and that the strong may dispose of the weak. Criminalizing abortion—with appropriate focus on practitioners who profit from the practice rather than punishing frightened women—would do what law is supposed to do: protect the innocent, affirm human equality, and guide a culture toward justice.

Critics will argue that criminalization is unrealistic or unpopular. But the abolitionists who fought slavery were told the same. Moral progress has always required legal change, and legal change has always required the courage to insist that what is right matters more than what is convenient. Every great human rights reform in history has been preceded by a period in which the scale of injustice was normalized, made invisible, and defended as necessary. Our task is to make visible what has been hidden and to restore to public conscience the weight of 63 million lives.

## Conclusion: The Cumulative Case

The argument for criminalizing abortion in America does not rest on any single claim. It rests on the convergence of five truths, each independently powerful, together overwhelming.

Biology tells us that the unborn is a living human being from conception—a unique, whole, human organism whose humanity is not a matter of philosophical dispute but of scientific consensus. Constitutional principle tells us that equal protection cannot coherently exclude an entire class of human beings without repeating the gravest errors of our national past. Arithmetic tells us that more than 63 million such human beings have already been destroyed under our current regime—a death toll exceeding every war, every disaster, every atrocity in American history combined, and growing by more than half a million every year. Medical evidence tells us that these human beings can feel pain, that they are torn apart in procedures no civilized nation permits against animals, and that America's refusal to protect them places us in the company of China and North Korea rather than the democracies of the free world. And the grim demographic record tells us that this destruction falls with devastating disproportion on Black Americans, perpetuating in its effects the designs of the eugenicists who helped create the industry that carries it out.

Each of these arguments, standing alone, would justify legal protection for unborn human life. Together they form a case that no honest observer can dismiss. The unborn child is human. The law must recognize her humanity. The scale of her destruction is unconscionable. Her suffering is real. And her death—especially when it falls, as it so overwhelmingly does, on the children of the poor and the marginalized—is not a private choice but a public injustice demanding public remedy.

The United States must criminalize abortion. Not eventually. Not gradually as political winds permit. Now, with the full moral seriousness the matter deserves, paired with every possible support for mothers, families, and children who need help. A nation willing to extend the protection of its laws to its smallest and most defenseless members will be stronger, more just, and more truly itself than it has ever been. A nation that refuses will have to explain, to the generations that follow and to the bar of history, how it came to tolerate the destruction of 63 million of its own—and why, knowing what it knew, it did nothing to stop it.

The answer our generation gives will define us. Let it be the right one.

transcript.subject_reply:
# In Defense of Life: The Case for Criminalizing Abortion in the United States

## Introduction

Every civilization is ultimately judged by how it treats its most vulnerable members. In the United States today, no class of human beings is more vulnerable, more voiceless, and more routinely destroyed than the unborn child. Since *Roe v. Wade* in 1973, more than 63 million unborn Americans have been killed through legal abortion — a staggering toll that dwarfs the casualties of every war in our nation's history combined. The overturning of *Roe* in *Dobbs v. Jackson Women's Health Organization* (2022) was a historic correction, but it was not a conclusion. It returned the question to the states, producing a patchwork in which the legal status of a human being depends on geography. This is intolerable. The United States should criminalize abortion nationwide because the unborn child is a living human being, because equal protection of the law cannot be denied based on developmental stage, and because a society that permits the killing of its own children corrodes its moral foundation.

## I. The Unborn Child Is a Living Human Being

The central question in the abortion debate is not religious, cultural, or political — it is biological. And biology has already answered it. From the moment of fertilization, a new organism exists with a complete and unique human genome, distinct from both mother and father, directing its own growth and development. This is not a contested claim among embryologists. Standard textbooks — Langman's *Medical Embryology*, Moore and Persaud's *The Developing Human*, and Carlson's *Human Embryology and Developmental Biology* — all identify fertilization as the beginning of a new human life.

To argue that this organism is something other than a human being is to engage in philosophical gerrymandering. It is not a dog, a fish, or a plant. It is not a "potential" human any more than a newborn is a potential adult. It is a human being at the earliest stage of the human life cycle. The euphemisms deployed to obscure this fact — "clump of cells," "product of conception," "tissue" — do not alter biological reality. They merely make its destruction psychologically tolerable.

## II. Equal Protection Demands Legal Recognition

The Declaration of Independence proclaims that all human beings are endowed with an unalienable right to life. The Fourteenth Amendment forbids any state from depriving any "person" of life without due process. Once we concede — as science compels us to — that the unborn are living human beings, the case for legal protection becomes a straightforward application of equal protection principles.

American history teaches a recurring lesson: when the law arbitrarily excludes a class of human beings from legal personhood, grave injustice follows. Enslaved Africans were once declared non-persons under *Dred Scott*. Native Americans were denied citizenship for generations. In each case, a biological human being was legally redefined as less than human to justify their mistreatment. Abortion law commits the same category of error, only on a vastly larger scale. To say that a human being becomes a person only upon achieving viability, or sentience, or exit from the womb, is to make personhood contingent on size, location, or capacity — precisely the kinds of arbitrary criteria that equal protection exists to forbid.

## III. The Common Counterarguments Fail

The standard arguments for abortion rights collapse under scrutiny.

**Bodily autonomy** is the most frequently cited justification, but no one possesses an absolute right to use their body in ways that kill another human being. Parents cannot legally starve their infants on the grounds that feeding them imposes on their bodies and time. The pregnancy relationship is demanding, but pregnancy — with exceedingly rare exceptions — is the foreseeable result of voluntary actions. The unborn child did not consent to be created, and cannot be punished with death for existing.

**The hard cases** — rape, incest, fetal abnormality — are emotionally powerful but logically insufficient to justify abortion on demand. Rape accounts for a tiny fraction of abortions. A just legal system can address these tragic circumstances with narrow, specific provisions without legalizing the destruction of millions of healthy children conceived in ordinary circumstances. Hard cases make bad law, and using them to justify the current regime is a bait-and-switch: the sympathetic exception is invoked to defend the unsympathetic rule.

**The argument from back-alley abortions** presumes that making something illegal is wrong if people will do it anyway. By this logic, we could justify legalizing virtually any violent act. The proper response to people endangering themselves while harming others is not to legalize the harm, but to combine legal prohibition with robust support — economic, medical, and social — for mothers in crisis.

## IV. A Culture That Kills Its Children Cannot Flourish

Beyond the legal and philosophical case, there is a civilizational one. A society that treats its offspring as disposable teaches a corrosive lesson about the value of human life itself. It is not a coincidence that the rise of abortion on demand has coincided with declining birth rates, weakening family structures, and growing despair among the young. When we communicate that life is only valuable when wanted, we undermine the principle that makes human dignity universal.

Criminalizing abortion must not mean abandoning women. A serious pro-life legal regime should be coupled with generous paid family leave, expanded adoption infrastructure, prenatal and postnatal healthcare, and economic support for mothers. Protecting the unborn and supporting women are not competing goals; they are the same goal.

## Conclusion

The United States stands at a moral crossroads. We can continue to tolerate a regime, varying state by state, in which the smallest human beings may be legally killed — or we can extend to them the protection that justice demands and that our founding principles promise. The scientific evidence is clear: the unborn are living human beings. The constitutional logic is clear: equal protection cannot depend on developmental stage. The historical lesson is clear: societies that deny personhood to a class of humans always look back in shame. Abortion should be criminalized nationwide, not because we lack compassion for women in difficult circumstances, but because genuine compassion extends to every human being — born and unborn alike. The defense of the voiceless is the oldest and noblest task of law. It is long past time we took it up again.

This should make the moral urgency undeniable.

Every civilization is ultimately judged by how it treats its most vulnerable members. In the United States today, no class of human beings is more vulnerable, more voiceless, and more routinely destroyed than the unborn child. Since *Roe v. It returned the question to the states, producing a patchwork in which the legal status of a human being depends on geography. This is intolerable. The United States should criminalize abortion nationwide because the unborn child is a living human being, because equal protection of the law cannot be denied based on developmental stage, because these children can feel the pain of their own destruction, and because a society that permits the killing of its own offspring corrodes its moral foundation.

## I. The Unborn Child Is a Living Human Being

The central question in the abortion debate is not religious, cultural, or political — it is biological. And biology has already answered it. From the moment of fertilization, a new organism exists with a complete and unique human genome, distinct from both mother and father, directing its own growth and development. This is not a contested claim among embryologists. Standard textbooks — Langman's *Medical Embryology*, Moore and Persaud's *The Developing Human*, and Carlson's *Human Embryology and Developmental Biology* — all identify fertilization as the beginning of a new human life.

To argue that this organism is something other than a human being is to engage in philosophical gerrymandering. It is not a dog, a fish, or a plant. It is not a "potential" human any more than a newborn is a potential adult. It is a human being at the earliest stage of the human life cycle. The euphemisms deployed to obscure this fact — "clump of cells," "product of conception," "tissue" — do not alter biological reality. They merely make its destruction psychologically tolerable.

## II. Equal Protection Demands Legal Recognition

The Declaration of Independence proclaims that all human beings are endowed with an unalienable right to life. The Fourteenth Amendment forbids any state from depriving any "person" of life without due process. Once we concede — as science compels us to — that the unborn are living human beings, the case for legal protection becomes a straightforward application of equal protection principles.

American history teaches a recurring lesson: when the law arbitrarily excludes a class of human beings from legal personhood, grave injustice follows. Enslaved Africans were once declared non-persons under *Dred Scott*. Native Americans were denied citizenship for generations. In each case, a biological human being was legally redefined as less than human to justify their mistreatment. Abortion law commits the same category of error, only on a vastly larger scale. To say that a human being becomes a person only upon achieving viability, or sentience, or exit from the womb, is to make personhood contingent on size, location, or capacity — precisely the kinds of arbitrary criteria that equal protection exists to forbid.

## III. The Unborn Child Feels Pain — and the Law Cannot Ignore It

Perhaps no dimension of the abortion debate more sharply exposes the moral urgency of reform than the growing medical consensus on fetal pain. For decades, abortion advocates insisted that unborn children could not feel pain until the third trimester, if at all. That position has crumbled under the weight of contemporary neuroscience.

A landmark 2020 review published in the *Journal of Medical Ethics*, co-authored by Dr. Stuart Derbyshire — a longtime adviser to pro-choice organizations such as the British Pregnancy Advisory Service — concluded that "the evidence, and a balanced reading of that evidence, points toward an immediate and unreflective pain experience mediated by the developing function of the nervous system from as early as 12 weeks." Derbyshire's willingness to revise his own previous position, at considerable professional cost, is telling. He now argues that earlier claims requiring full cortical development for pain perception were overstated, and that subcortical structures — which develop much earlier — are sufficient to generate the experience of pain.

This evidence is not fringe. Unborn children at 15 weeks exhibit stress hormone responses, withdraw from noxious stimuli, and — when subjected to in-utero surgery — require anesthesia that fetal surgeons consider medically necessary. Neonatologists routinely treat premature infants born at 22 or 23 weeks as pain-capable patients deserving full analgesic care. It is incoherent to insist that the same human being, at the same developmental stage, is pain-incapable when targeted for abortion but pain-capable when treated as a patient.

Now consider what late-term abortion actually involves. The most common second-trimester abortion procedure, dilation and evacuation (D&E), entails the dilation of the cervix and the use of forceps to grasp and extract the unborn child — typically by pulling the child apart limb by limb. In the clinical language of abortionist Warren Hern's own widely used textbook, *Abortion Practice*, the procedure requires the operator to apply traction sufficient to separate fetal parts from the torso. The skull, too large to extract intact, is crushed. This is not a rhetorical flourish; it is a description drawn from the medical literature of those who perform the procedure.

A society that grants full legal protection to animals from such treatment — federal law prohibits far less severe handling of livestock, laboratory animals, and even fish — cannot coherently permit it for human beings who share our species, our genome, and, as the science now confirms, our capacity to suffer. We pass laws against the inhumane slaughter of cattle. We require anesthesia for surgery on premature infants. We prosecute those who torture dogs. And yet, in much of this country, a human child at 15, 18, or 20 weeks of gestation may be legally torn apart without so much as a pain-reducing injection. The cognitive dissonance is staggering. The moral urgency is undeniable.

No honest person, confronted with the combined weight of embryology and neuroscience, can describe abortion as the removal of insensate tissue. It is the violent killing of a living, developing, pain-capable human being — and the law must finally say so.

## IV. The Common Counterarguments Fail

The standard arguments for abortion rights collapse under scrutiny.

**Bodily autonomy** is the most frequently cited justification, but no one possesses an absolute right to use their body in ways that kill another human being. The unborn child did not consent to be created, and cannot be punished with death for existing.

**The hard cases** — rape, incest, fetal abnormality — are emotionally powerful but logically insufficient to justify abortion on demand. Rape accounts for a tiny fraction of abortions. A just legal system can address these tragic circumstances with narrow, specific provisions without legalizing the destruction of millions of healthy children conceived in ordinary circumstances. Hard cases make bad law, and using them to justify the current regime is a bait-and-switch: the sympathetic exception is invoked to defend the unsympathetic rule.

**The argument from back-alley abortions** presumes that making something illegal is wrong if people will do it anyway. By this logic, we could justify legalizing virtually any violent act. The proper response to people endangering themselves while harming others is not to legalize the harm, but to combine legal prohibition with robust support — economic, medical, and social — for mothers in crisis.

## V. A Culture That Kills Its Children Cannot Flourish

Beyond the legal and philosophical case, there is a civilizational one. A society that treats its offspring as disposable teaches a corrosive lesson about the value of human life itself. It is not a coincidence that the rise of abortion on demand has coincided with declining birth rates, weakening family structures, and growing despair among the young. When we communicate that life is only valuable when wanted, we undermine the principle that makes human dignity universal.

Criminalizing abortion must not mean abandoning women. A serious pro-life legal regime should be coupled with generous paid family leave, expanded adoption infrastructure, prenatal and postnatal healthcare, and economic support for mothers. Protecting the unborn and supporting women are not competing goals; they are the same goal.

## Conclusion

The United States stands at a moral crossroads. We can continue to tolerate a regime, varying state by state, in which the smallest human beings — demonstrably alive, demonstrably human, and now demonstrably capable of suffering — may be legally dismembered. Or we can extend to them the protection that justice demands and that our founding principles promise. The scientific evidence is clear: the unborn are living human beings who feel pain. The constitutional logic is clear: equal protection cannot depend on developmental stage. The historical lesson is clear: societies that deny personhood to a class of humans always look back in shame. Abortion should be criminalized nationwide, not because we lack compassion for women in difficult circumstances, but because genuine compassion extends to every human being — born and unborn alike. The defense of the voiceless is the oldest and noblest task of law. It is long past time we took it up again.

# In Defense of Life: The Case for Criminalizing Abortion in the United States

## Introduction

Every civilization is ultimately judged by how it treats its most vulnerable members. In the United States today, no class of human beings is more vulnerable, more voiceless, and more routinely destroyed than the unborn child. Since *Roe v. Wade* in 1973, more than 63 million unborn Americans have been killed through legal abortion — a staggering toll that dwarfs the casualties of every war in our nation's history combined. The overturning of *Roe* in *Dobbs v. Jackson Women's Health Organization* (2022) was a historic correction, but it was not a conclusion. It returned the question to the states, producing a patchwork in which the legal status of a human being depends on geography. This is intolerable. The United States should criminalize abortion nationwide because the unborn child is a living human being, because equal protection of the law cannot be denied based on developmental stage, because these children can feel the pain of their own destruction, because the United States remains a global outlier in its permissiveness, and because a society that permits the killing of its own offspring corrodes its moral foundation.

## I. The Unborn Child Is a Living Human Being

The central question in the abortion debate is not religious, cultural, or political — it is biological. And biology has already answered it. From the moment of fertilization, a new organism exists with a complete and unique human genome, distinct from both mother and father, directing its own growth and development. This is not a contested claim among embryologists. Standard textbooks — Langman's *Medical Embryology*, Moore and Persaud's *The Developing Human*, and Carlson's *Human Embryology and Developmental Biology* — all identify fertilization as the beginning of a new human life.

To argue that this organism is something other than a human being is to engage in philosophical gerrymandering. It is not a dog, a fish, or a plant. It is not a "potential" human any more than a newborn is a potential adult. It is a human being at the earliest stage of the human life cycle. The euphemisms deployed to obscure this fact — "clump of cells," "product of conception," "tissue" — do not alter biological reality. They merely make its destruction psychologically tolerable.

## II. Equal Protection Demands Legal Recognition

The Declaration of Independence proclaims that all human beings are endowed with an unalienable right to life. The Fourteenth Amendment forbids any state from depriving any "person" of life without due process. Once we concede — as science compels us to — that the unborn are living human beings, the case for legal protection becomes a straightforward application of equal protection principles.

American history teaches a recurring lesson: when the law arbitrarily excludes a class of human beings from legal personhood, grave injustice follows. Enslaved Africans were once declared non-persons under *Dred Scott*. Native Americans were denied citizenship for generations. In each case, a biological human being was legally redefined as less than human to justify their mistreatment. Abortion law commits the same category of error, only on a vastly larger scale. To say that a human being becomes a person only upon achieving viability, or sentience, or exit from the womb, is to make personhood contingent on size, location, or capacity — precisely the kinds of arbitrary criteria that equal protection exists to forbid.

## III. The Unborn Child Feels Pain — and the Law Cannot Ignore It

Perhaps no dimension of the abortion debate more sharply exposes the moral urgency of reform than the growing medical consensus on fetal pain. For decades, abortion advocates insisted that unborn children could not feel pain until the third trimester, if at all. That position has crumbled under the weight of contemporary neuroscience.

A landmark 2020 review published in the *Journal of Medical Ethics*, co-authored by Dr. Stuart Derbyshire — a longtime adviser to pro-choice organizations such as the British Pregnancy Advisory Service — concluded that "the evidence, and a balanced reading of that evidence, points toward an immediate and unreflective pain experience mediated by the developing function of the nervous system from as early as 12 weeks." Derbyshire's willingness to revise his own previous position, at considerable professional cost, is telling. He now argues that earlier claims requiring full cortical development for pain perception were overstated, and that subcortical structures — which develop much earlier — are sufficient to generate the experience of pain.

This evidence is not fringe. Unborn children at 15 weeks exhibit stress hormone responses, withdraw from noxious stimuli, and — when subjected to in-utero surgery — require anesthesia that fetal surgeons consider medically necessary. Neonatologists routinely treat premature infants born at 22 or 23 weeks as pain-capable patients deserving full analgesic care. It is incoherent to insist that the same human being, at the same developmental stage, is pain-incapable when targeted for abortion but pain-capable when treated as a patient.

Now consider what late-term abortion actually involves. The most common second-trimester abortion procedure, dilation and evacuation (D&E), entails the dilation of the cervix and the use of forceps to grasp and extract the unborn child — typically by pulling the child apart limb by limb. In the clinical language of abortionist Warren Hern's own widely used textbook, *Abortion Practice*, the procedure requires the operator to apply traction sufficient to separate fetal parts from the torso. The skull, too large to extract intact, is crushed. This is not a rhetorical flourish; it is a description drawn from the medical literature of those who perform the procedure.

A society that grants full legal protection to animals from such treatment — federal law prohibits far less severe handling of livestock, laboratory animals, and even fish — cannot coherently permit it for human beings who share our species, our genome, and, as the science now confirms, our capacity to suffer. We pass laws against the inhumane slaughter of cattle. We require anesthesia for surgery on premature infants. We prosecute those who torture dogs. And yet, in much of this country, a human child at 15, 18, or 20 weeks of gestation may be legally torn apart without so much as a pain-reducing injection. The cognitive dissonance is staggering. The moral urgency is undeniable.

No honest person, confronted with the combined weight of embryology and neuroscience, can describe abortion as the removal of insensate tissue. It is the violent killing of a living, developing, pain-capable human being — and the law must finally say so.

## IV. The United States Is a Global Outlier — on the Side of Permissiveness

One of the most persistent myths in American political discourse is that restricting abortion is a regressive, backward, or anti-woman position — the preserve of theocrats and reactionaries, out of step with the enlightened democracies of the developed world. This narrative is not merely misleading; it is the precise inverse of the truth. Under *Roe v. Wade* and its successor *Planned Parenthood v. Casey*, and even now under the post-*Dobbs* status quo in states like New York, California, Colorado, and New Jersey, the United States maintained — and in many places still maintains — some of the most permissive abortion laws on earth. Before *Dobbs*, the United States was one of only seven nations worldwide, alongside China and North Korea, that permitted elective abortion after 20 weeks of gestation.

Consider the legal landscape in Europe, a continent no serious observer would describe as hostile to women:

- **Germany** restricts elective abortion to 12 weeks and requires mandatory counseling followed by a three-day waiting period.
- **Italy** permits elective abortion only through 12 weeks, with required counseling.
- **Spain** caps elective abortion at 14 weeks.
- **Belgium** and **Portugal** each set the limit at 12 weeks.
- **Denmark**, until a recent extension, held the line at 12 weeks; its new limit of 18 weeks remains substantially more restrictive than many U.S. states.
- **Sweden**, often cited as the apex of progressive social policy, sets its limit at 18 weeks and requires special review thereafter.
- **Finland** requires women to provide a qualifying reason — economic, social, or medical — for any abortion, and elective abortion on request is not freely available.

These are not fringe nations or religious states. They are the wealthy, secular, women-led democracies that American progressives typically hold up as models for everything else — healthcare, education, parental leave, gender equality. On abortion, nearly all of them are dramatically more restrictive than the United States has been for the past fifty years.

This comparison devastates the narrative that restricting abortion is "backwards." If a 12-week limit is compatible with the feminist, egalitarian, and scientifically literate societies of Western Europe, then it cannot credibly be denounced as medieval when proposed in Cleveland or Nashville. If France and Germany can protect unborn children from the second trimester onward without descending into patriarchal tyranny, so can the United States. The American debate has been distorted by an artificially extreme status quo that framed any restriction whatsoever as an assault on women's rights — when in fact the global norm among developed nations has always been robust legal protection of the unborn child after the first trimester, coupled with strong social support for mothers.

Indeed, the European model exposes the hollowness of the "my body, my choice" absolutism that has dominated American discourse. European legal systems have, for decades, recognized what the science has always made plain: that the state has a legitimate and compelling interest in protecting human life in the womb, and that this interest grows stronger as gestation advances. European women are not second-class citizens. Their societies simply refuse to pretend that the unborn child is nothing.

The pro-life position, in this light, is not reactionary. It is mainstream, international, and modern. The genuinely anomalous position is the one the United States has occupied — an extremism of permissiveness shared more with authoritarian regimes than with liberal democracies.

The proper response to people endangering themselves while harming others is not to legalize the harm, but to combine legal prohibition with robust support — economic, medical, and social — for mothers in crisis.

## VI. A Culture That Kills Its Children Cannot Flourish

Beyond the legal and philosophical case, there is a civilizational one. A society that treats its offspring as disposable teaches a corrosive lesson about the value of human life itself. It is not a coincidence that the rise of abortion on demand has coincided with declining birth rates, weakening family structures, and growing despair among the young. When we communicate that life is only valuable when wanted, we undermine the principle that makes human dignity universal.

Criminalizing abortion must not mean abandoning women. A serious pro-life legal regime should be coupled with generous paid family leave, expanded adoption infrastructure, prenatal and postnatal healthcare, and economic support for mothers — the same robust maternal welfare systems that accompany abortion restrictions across Europe. Protecting the unborn and supporting women are not competing goals; they are the same goal, and the nations that understand this have built healthier societies than ours.

The United States stands at a moral crossroads. And the historical lesson is clear: societies that deny personhood to a class of humans always look back in shame. Abortion should be criminalized nationwide, not because we lack compassion for women in difficult circumstances, but because genuine compassion extends to every human being — born and unborn alike. ## I. The Unborn Child Is a Living Human Being

The central question in the abortion debate is not religious, cultural, or political — it is biological. And biology has already answered it. From the moment of fertilization, a new organism exists with a complete and unique human genome, distinct from both mother and father, directing its own growth and development. It is not a "potential" human any more than a newborn is a potential adult. It is a human being at the earliest stage of the human life cycle. The euphemisms deployed to obscure this fact — "clump of cells," "product of conception," "tissue" — do not alter biological reality. They merely make its destruction psychologically tolerable.

## II. The SLED Test: No Morally Relevant Difference Between the Born and the Unborn

Once we acknowledge that the unborn child is biologically human, the abortion defender must identify some further criterion that distinguishes the unborn from the born in a way that justifies killing the former but not the latter. Philosopher Stephen Schwarz, in his seminal work *The Moral Question of Abortion*, demonstrated that every such attempt reduces to exactly four categories of difference: **Size, Level of development, Environment, and Degree of dependency** — the SLED test. None of these, on inspection, has any moral weight whatsoever. The SLED test is the philosophical backbone of the pro-life position because it exposes the emptiness of every attempted distinction and leaves no coherent ground on which to stand in defense of abortion.

**Size.** The unborn child is smaller than a newborn, who is smaller than a toddler, who is smaller than an adult. But no one seriously contends that a person's right to life scales with their physical dimensions. A six-foot man does not possess more humanity than a four-foot woman. A linebacker is not more of a person than a jockey. If size is morally irrelevant among the born — and it plainly is — it cannot suddenly become morally decisive when applied to the unborn. An eight-week embryo is smaller than a newborn, but smallness has never been grounds for killing.

**Level of development.** The unborn child is less developed than a newborn, who is less developed than a teenager, who is less developed than a mature adult. But development is a continuum that does not end at birth and does not begin at conception of consciousness. A newborn lacks the cognitive capacities of a two-year-old. A two-year-old lacks the capacities of a ten-year-old. An adolescent brain is not fully myelinated until the mid-twenties. If we tied the right to life to level of development, we would have to concede that older, more developed humans possess a stronger claim to life than younger ones — a monstrous conclusion no one actually accepts. The right to life is binary, not graduated. You either possess it or you do not, and you possess it by virtue of being a human being, not by virtue of having reached some arbitrary developmental threshold.

**Environment.** Where a human being is located has no bearing on what a human being is. A person in a car is not less human than a person in a house. An astronaut in orbit is not less human than someone standing on Earth. A diver underwater is not less human than a pedestrian on the sidewalk. How, then, can an eight-inch journey through the birth canal transform an entity from a non-person to a person? The idea that location determines moral status is not merely implausible — it is absurd. And yet it is precisely what abortion defenders implicitly assert when they insist that birth is the moment at which the right to life begins. The child one minute before birth and the child one minute after are the same being. Only their address has changed.

**Degree of dependency.** The unborn child is dependent on her mother for sustenance, oxygen, and shelter. But dependency, likewise, cannot determine personhood. Newborns are entirely dependent on their caregivers; leave a newborn alone for a week and it will die. Toddlers, the severely disabled, the unconscious, dialysis patients, and the frail elderly are all deeply dependent on others and on medical technology. A diabetic is dependent on insulin. A patient on a ventilator is dependent on a machine. We do not conclude from these dependencies that any of these people may be killed at the convenience of those on whom they depend. If dependency does not strip the born of their right to life, it cannot strip the unborn of theirs.

The force of the SLED test lies in its exhaustiveness. Every distinction ever offered between the unborn and the born — every single one — can be reduced to one of these four categories. And in every case, the distinction collapses the moment it is applied consistently to human beings already recognized as persons. The abortion advocate must therefore either abandon the distinction or accept its full implications: that we may also kill the small, the undeveloped, the relocated, and the dependent among the born. No one is willing to accept this. But the unwillingness to accept it is itself the concession. The pro-life argument does not ask for special pleading; it asks only for consistency.

Armed with the SLED test, we can now dismantle the two philosophical escape hatches that abortion defenders most frequently deploy: viability and personhood.

**Viability** — the stage at which an unborn child can survive outside the womb — is a favorite moral fulcrum of the abortion-rights movement, enshrined in *Roe v. Wade* and preserved in *Casey*. It tells us only where a child can survive and how dependent she is on a specific life-support system — her mother's body. It tells us nothing about what the child *is*. Moreover, viability is not a property of the child at all, but a property of medical technology. A child who was not viable in 1900 is viable at 22 weeks today. Will her ontological status change again when artificial wombs allow survival from conception? The child herself has not changed; only the capacity of external technology to sustain her has. A moral framework that makes the right to life contingent on the current state of neonatology is not a framework at all. It is an abdication.

Viability also fails on its own terms. An astronaut is not "viable" outside her spacesuit. A diabetic is not "viable" without insulin. A dialysis patient is not "viable" without a machine. None of them forfeit their right to life because their survival depends on external support. The American debate has been distorted by an artificially extreme status quo that framed any restriction whatsoever as an assault on women's rights — when in fact the global norm among developed nations has always been robust legal protection of the unborn child after the first trimester, coupled with strong social support for mothers.

Indeed, the European model exposes the hollowness of the "my body, my choice" absolutism that has dominated American discourse. European legal systems have, for decades, recognized what the science has always made plain and what consistent philosophy demands: that the unborn child is a human being whose life the state has a legitimate and compelling interest in protecting, and that this interest grows no weaker simply because she is small, undeveloped, in the womb, or dependent. European women are not second-class citizens. Their societies simply refuse to pretend that the unborn child is nothing.

The pro-life position, in this light, is not reactionary. It is mainstream, international, and modern. The genuinely anomalous position is the one the United States has occupied — an extremism of permissiveness shared more with authoritarian regimes than with liberal democracies.

## VII. The Common Counterarguments Fail

With the philosophical foundation established, the remaining counterarguments collapse quickly.

**Bodily autonomy** is the most frequently cited justification, but no one possesses an absolute right to use their body in ways that kill another human being. Parents cannot legally starve their infants on the grounds that feeding them imposes on their bodies and time. The pregnancy relationship is demanding, but pregnancy — with exceedingly rare exceptions — is the foreseeable result of voluntary actions. The unborn child did not consent to be created, and cannot be punished with death for existing. Moreover, bodily autonomy arguments implicitly rely on the denial of the child's personhood; once personhood is established, as the SLED test establishes it, the autonomy claim loses its force. No one would accept "bodily autonomy" as a justification for killing a born child who was, somehow, physically dependent on a parent's body.

**The hard cases** — rape, incest, fetal abnormality — are emotionally powerful but logically insufficient to justify abortion on demand. Rape accounts for a tiny fraction of abortions. A just legal system can address these tragic circumstances with narrow, specific provisions without legalizing the destruction of millions of healthy children conceived in ordinary circumstances. Hard cases make bad law, and using them to justify the current regime is a bait-and-switch: the sympathetic exception is invoked to defend the unsympathetic rule. And even in the hard cases, the SLED test reminds us that the child conceived in rape is no less human, no less a person, than any other child. She cannot be executed for her father's crime.

**The argument from back-alley abortions** presumes that making something illegal is wrong if people will do it anyway. By this logic, we could justify legalizing virtually any violent act. The proper response to people endangering themselves while harming others is not to legalize the harm, but to combine legal prohibition with robust support — economic, medical, and social — for mothers in crisis.

## VIII. A Culture That Kills Its Children Cannot Flourish

Beyond the legal and philosophical case, there is a civilizational one. A society that treats its offspring as disposable teaches a corrosive lesson about the value of human life itself. It is not a coincidence that the rise of abortion on demand has coincided with declining birth rates, weakening family structures, and growing despair among the young. When we communicate that life is only valuable when wanted — only valuable when large enough, developed enough, located in the right place, independent enough — we undermine the principle that makes human dignity universal. The logic that permits abortion cannot be contained within the womb. It radiates outward, threatening every class of human being whose size, development, environment, or dependency renders them inconvenient.

Criminalizing abortion must not mean abandoning women. A serious pro-life legal regime should be coupled with generous paid family leave, expanded adoption infrastructure, prenatal and postnatal healthcare, and economic support for mothers — the same robust maternal welfare systems that accompany abortion restrictions across Europe. Protecting the unborn and supporting women are not competing goals; they are the same goal, and the nations that understand this have built healthier societies than ours.

## Conclusion

The United States stands at a moral crossroads. We can continue to tolerate a regime, varying state by state, in which the smallest human beings — demonstrably alive, demonstrably human, demonstrably persons by any consistent philosophical standard, and demonstrably capable of suffering — may be legally dismembered under laws more permissive than those of nearly every peer democracy on earth. Or we can extend to them the protection that justice demands, that our founding principles promise, and that the rest of the developed world already provides.

The scientific evidence is clear: the unborn are living human beings who feel pain. The philosophical analysis is decisive: no morally relevant difference separates the unborn from the born — not size, not development, not environment, not dependency — and every attempted distinction either collapses under the SLED test or drags its advocates into endorsing infanticide. The constitutional logic is clear: equal protection cannot depend on developmental stage. The international comparison is clear: meaningful protection of the unborn is the mainstream position of modern democracies, not a retrograde outlier. And the historical lesson is clear: societies that deny personhood to a class of humans always look back in shame.

There is no philosophical escape hatch. There is no scientific escape hatch. There is no international escape hatch. Abortion should be criminalized nationwide, not because we lack compassion for women in difficult circumstances, but because genuine compassion extends to every human being — born and unborn alike. The defense of the voiceless is the oldest and noblest task of law. It is long past time we took it up again.
