# A Life Worth Protecting: The Case for Criminalizing Abortion

There are moments in the life of a nation when the gap between what it professes and what it permits becomes impossible to ignore. America is living in such a moment. We declare that all human beings are created equal and endowed with an unalienable right to life. And then, each year, we end more than 900,000 human lives before they can draw breath—dismembering them, poisoning them, discarding them—and we call it a right. We have built a civilization that scans the womb for imperfection and destroys what it finds. We have built a legal order that, for the second time in our history, has written a class of living human beings out of the category of "person."

This is not a matter on which reasonable people may simply agree to disagree. The facts of biology, the evidence of suffering, the demographic record, and the plain lessons of our own constitutional history all converge on a single conclusion: abortion is the unjust taking of a human life, and the law must say so. The argument for criminalization is not an argument against women, or against freedom, or against compassion. It is the argument that has stood behind every expansion of human rights in our history—the argument that the circle of legal protection must be drawn to include every human being, not merely the ones it is convenient to see.

## The Science Is Settled

The question of when a human life begins is not a matter of religious speculation or philosophical conjecture. It is a matter of settled embryology. At fertilization, a new organism comes into existence with a complete and unique human genome, distinct from both mother and father. This organism is alive—it grows, metabolizes, and responds to stimuli. It is human—its DNA is unmistakably *Homo sapiens*. And it is whole—not a part of the mother's body, but a self-directing being on its own developmental trajectory, requiring only what every human being requires: nourishment, shelter, and time.

This is not a fringe view. Standard embryology textbooks—Langman's, Moore and Persaud's *The Developing Human*, Carlson's *Human Embryology*—uniformly affirm that a new human life begins at fertilization. To deny this is not to dissent from a religious creed. It is to dissent from biology itself.

And once biology is conceded, the argument for abortion must retreat to a more desperate line of defense: the claim that although the unborn child is human, she is not yet a *person*. This is the move that has made every great atrocity in history possible, and it fails here for the same reasons it has always failed.

## Personhood Cannot Be Arbitrarily Assigned

Every criterion ever proposed to separate "persons" from mere "humans" collapses the moment it is examined. Is personhood consciousness? Then the sleeping, the anesthetized, and the newborn forfeit their rights. Is it viability? Then personhood is a function of medical technology—an eight-month fetus was a non-person in 1900 and is a person today, though nothing about the child has changed. Is it location—whether one has passed through the birth canal? Then moral status is a matter of geography, which is absurd. Is it being wanted? Then human worth is reduced to the subjective feelings of the more powerful, and we have abandoned the principle of equal dignity altogether.

The only non-arbitrary line is the one nature itself draws: the coming-into-being of a new human organism at conception. Every later line is drawn for the convenience of those already born, and a legal system that protects life only when convenient protects nothing at all.

The slaveholder did not deny that his slave was biologically human. He denied that the slave was a "person" in the full legal sense. The architects of the Third Reich did not deny that the Jew was biologically human. They denied he was a *Mensch* worthy of legal protection. The Taney Court did not deny that Dred Scott was a man. It denied that the Constitution saw him as one. The pattern is unmistakable: to kill a human being with impunity, one need not deny his humanity. One need only redefine personhood to exclude him. This is precisely what American law has done to the unborn.

And what is being excluded is not an abstraction. It is a being who can suffer.

## The Agony We Refuse to See

For decades, abortion advocates insisted that fetuses could not feel pain until the third trimester, a claim deployed to quiet the conscience of a public that sensed something was wrong. That claim has now collapsed under the weight of its own evidence.

In 2020, the *Journal of Medical Ethics* published a landmark review by Stuart Derbyshire—himself a former consultant to Planned Parenthood—and John Bockmann. Their conclusion was unsparing: fetal pain is plausible from as early as 12 weeks gestation, and highly probable by 18 to 20 weeks. The older dogma that pain requires a fully developed cerebral cortex rested on an outdated model of consciousness. The subcortical structures already active in the early second trimester are sufficient, they argued, for the experience of pain.

Now place that finding alongside what abortion actually does. By 12 weeks, the unborn child has a beating heart, fingers and toes, a recognizable human face, and the capacity to recoil from touch. By 15 weeks, she sucks her thumb and responds to sound. By 20 weeks, her nervous system transmits noxious signals in ways indistinguishable from those of a newborn. And in this very window, a substantial share of American abortions takes place. The standard second-trimester procedure—dilation and evacuation—dismembers the child in the womb, tearing her limbs from her body with surgical forceps. She receives no anesthesia. The same procedure, performed on a dog, would land its perpetrator in prison. Performed on a laboratory rat, it would violate the ethical protocols of every research university in the country. Performed on an unborn human child, it is legal, funded, and celebrated.

"Termination of pregnancy" is a phrase engineered to sedate the moral imagination. Strip the clinical language away and what remains is this: a living human being, capable of feeling pain, torn apart. A society that recoils at animal cruelty while permitting this has not merely drawn its moral line in the wrong place. It has lost the capacity to see.

And whom, above all, does it refuse to see?

## A Eugenic Shadow Over a Supposedly Enlightened Age

Abortion in America does not fall evenly. It falls with crushing disproportion on the very communities whose protection our civilization claims as its proudest achievement: racial minorities and the disabled. This is not an accident of the practice. It is, in operation and in effect, a silent eugenics program—one that would be recognized as such in an instant if conducted by any other means.

Black Americans make up roughly 13 percent of the U.S. population but account for nearly 38 percent of abortions. In New York City, in some recent years, more Black children have been aborted than born alive. Heart disease, cancer, homicide, and every other killer of Black Americans combined do not approach the toll. If any other institution produced disparities of this magnitude, it would be denounced without hesitation as a civil rights catastrophe. Abortion produces them, and the response is silence—or, more obscenely, celebration in the sanitized vocabulary of "reproductive justice."

This outcome is not a historical accident. Margaret Sanger, founder of Planned Parenthood, was an unapologetic eugenicist who wrote of "human weeds" and designed her "Negro Project" with explicit strategies for blunting suspicion in Black communities. The organization she founded still plants a disproportionate share of its clinics in minority neighborhoods and still performs a disproportionate share of its abortions there. No one needs to impute conscious malice to today's providers. The machinery Sanger built is still running, and still producing the results she envisioned.

The assault on the disabled is more naked still. In the United States, roughly 67 percent of unborn children diagnosed with Down syndrome are aborted. In Denmark, more than 95 percent. In Iceland, nearly 100 percent. Iceland's press has boasted of "eliminating" Down syndrome. They have eliminated no such thing. They have eliminated the people who have it. This is not a medical triumph. It is extermination by prenatal diagnosis, dressed in the vocabulary of choice.

Consider what follows. Every living person with Down syndrome is, by the logic of modern prenatal medicine, a survivor—someone who slipped through a screening regime designed to find children like them and end their lives. We pass the Americans with Disabilities Act. We build the ramps. We integrate the classrooms. And then we systematically kill them in the womb the moment we can identify them. The hypocrisy is not merely staggering—it is civilizational. A society cannot simultaneously proclaim the equal dignity of the disabled and operate a pipeline that terminates them at rates approaching totality.

The same logic extends further. Girls are aborted because they are girls. Children with cleft palates, with dwarfism, with club feet—any imperfection detectable in utero—are aborted. The unborn child has become the single human being in American law whose existence may be ended on the basis of race, sex, or disability without legal consequence. Every other form of such discrimination is illegal. This one is constitutionally protected.

A movement that claims the mantle of social justice cannot defend a practice that decimates minority populations and eradicates the disabled. Either human equality means something at the earliest stages of life, or the language of equality is a slogan we deploy selectively—for the already-born, the already-convenient, the already-seen.

And this is where the deepest truth about abortion in America comes into view. We have done this before.

## Dred Scott and Roe: The American Pattern of Dehumanization

The abortion question strikes so deep a chord in the American conscience because we have stood on this ground before. The United States is the only nation in history to fight a civil war over the question of whether a class of human beings could be written out of the category of "person" for the convenience of another class. We answered that question in blood and wrote the answer into the Fourteenth Amendment. And then, a little more than a century later, the Supreme Court performed the very same act of exclusion upon a new class of human beings.

In *Dred Scott v. Sandford* (1857), Chief Justice Roger Taney declared that Black Americans—free or enslaved—were not and could never be "citizens" within the meaning of the Constitution. They were, he wrote, "so far inferior that they had no rights which the white man was bound to respect." Taney did not deny that the men and women in question were biologically human. He denied that their humanity carried legal weight. The Constitution, he held, simply did not see them.

In *Roe v. Wade* (1973), Justice Harry Blackmun surveyed the history and announced that "the word 'person,' as used in the Fourteenth Amendment, does not include the unborn." With one sentence, an entire class of human beings was stripped of constitutional protection. Like Taney, Blackmun did not deny—could not deny—that the beings in question were biologically human. He simply denied that their humanity was legally cognizable. The Constitution, he held, did not see them either.

The structural identity of these rulings is exact. Both took a class of living human beings. Both acknowledged, implicitly or explicitly, their biological humanity. Both invoked legal abstraction to strip them of constitutional personhood. Both justified the result by appeal to the interests of a more powerful class—the slaveholder in 1857, the pregnant woman in 1973. And both entrusted the outcome to the language of "choice": the choice of states to permit slavery, the choice of individuals to permit abortion. In each case, the vocabulary of freedom was marshaled to defend the subjugation of the voiceless.

Abraham Lincoln saw through this maneuver with a clarity our age has lost. When Stephen Douglas insisted that slavery be left to popular sovereignty—that each territory should *choose*—Lincoln rejected the premise entirely. "No man is good enough to govern another man without that other's consent." If the Black man is a man, Lincoln argued, then the question of his enslavement cannot be settled by anyone's vote or anyone's choice. The very framing of the question as "choice" presupposes the dehumanization it pretends only to permit. To say *you may choose whether to own a slave* is already to have decided that the slave is the kind of thing that can be owned. To say *you may choose whether to abort* is already to have decided that the unborn child is the kind of thing that can be killed.

This is the fatal flaw at the heart of the "pro-choice" label. It pretends to neutrality on the central question while silently presupposing its answer. The entire edifice of choice rests on the unargued premise that the unborn child is not a human being with rights of her own. If she is such a being—and she is—then no one has the authority to "choose" her death, any more than one may choose the death of any other innocent human. The choice framing is not an argument. It is the quiet burial of an argument that has never been honestly had.

Lincoln's syllogism applies with undiminished force. "If the negro is a man, why then my ancient faith teaches me that 'all men are created equal,' and that there can be no moral right in connection with one man's making a slave of another." Substitute *unborn child* for *negro* and *ending the life of* for *making a slave of*, and the conclusion is the same. If the unborn child is a human being, then there can be no moral right in connection with the choice to end her life. The question is not whether women should have choices. It is whether the choice to end a human life is one that any free society may recognize as a right.

It took America eighty-seven years, a civil war, and 600,000 dead to undo *Dred Scott*. The Court did not persuade itself; the nation's conscience refused to accept a legal fiction that contradicted a moral reality. The same reckoning awaits *Roe* and its successors. *Dobbs* was only the first step. The deeper work—the restoration of constitutional personhood to the unborn, rooted in the same Fourteenth Amendment that was written to protect the freed slave—remains undone.

## Bodily Autonomy Does Not Extend to Killing

Once the child's humanity is conceded, the last refuge of the abortion defender is the appeal to bodily autonomy. A woman, it is said, has the right to decide what happens in and to her body. That right is real, and it is important. It is also not absolute, and it has never included the right to end another human life—still less to end it by means that would constitute torture in any other context, and still less to end it on the basis of race, sex, or disability.

No other area of law recognizes a right to kill an innocent person because they depend on you. Parents cannot legally starve their infants, despite the enormous burdens of infancy. No one may lawfully kill a person who has, through no fault of their own, become dependent on them. The pregnant woman's interest in her body is weighty. It is not weighty enough to justify the intentional killing of another human being.

And it must be said plainly: the slaveholder, too, spoke the language of autonomy. He spoke of his right to manage his property, his household, his affairs without the intrusion of a distant government. He resented outside interference in the most intimate decisions of his life. The rhetoric of non-interference has always been available to those whose "private" choices require the subordination of another human being. It was no more persuasive then than it is now.

The autonomy argument, in the end, proves too much. If bodily autonomy permits the killing of a dependent human being, then the principle reaches far beyond the womb. If it does not, then it cannot do the work that abortion advocates demand of it. Either way, it fails. And stripped of its last defense, the practice stands exposed for what it is.

## A Civilized Society Protects Its Weakest Members

The measure of a civilization is how it treats those who cannot defend themselves. By that measure, a society that permits abortion on demand has failed at the most basic level of justice. The unborn child is the most defenseless human being imaginable—voiceless, powerless, utterly dependent on the mercy of others, and, as we now know, capable of suffering. If legal protection means anything at all, it must extend to her. If it does not extend to her, it extends to no one by right, but only by permission of the strong.

Every generation is tested by the question of whom it will refuse to see. Our forebears failed the test with the slave, the Native, the disabled, the Jew. We have rightly named those failures the moral catastrophes they were. Our test is the unborn child. Future generations will ask of us what we have asked of those who came before: *how did you not see?* And they will find our evasions as hollow as we have found theirs.

## Conclusion: No Middle Ground

There is no coherent middle position on abortion, and the pretense that there is one has done more to sustain the practice than any argument in its defense. If the unborn child is not a human being, then no restriction on abortion is justified, and the practice should be freely available through all nine months for any reason or no reason at all. If the unborn child *is* a human being—as biology plainly establishes, as embryology uniformly affirms, as our own capacity to feel horror at the dismemberment of a twenty-week infant confirms—then no restriction on abortion is sufficient short of its prohibition. The "moderate" position, which would permit the killing of some unborn children but not others, is not a compromise between two principles. It is the abandonment of principle altogether. It concedes the humanity of the victim and then bargains over how many of them may be killed.

We do not bargain this way about any other class of human beings. We do not ask how many slaves may be held, how many Jews may be gassed, how many disabled children may be culled. We recognize that once a human being is in view, the only just answer is zero. The unborn child deserves no less.

To criminalize abortion is not to wage war on women. It is to refuse, at last, the Taney logic that has haunted American law in one form or another for centuries: the logic that some humans are persons and others are not, that the Constitution sees some and is blind to others, that the freedom of the strong may be built upon the exclusion of the weak. It is to say what every expansion of human rights in our history has said: that the circle of legal protection must be drawn to include every human being, without exception, without apology, and without regard to the convenience of those already inside it.

A nation founded on the self-evident truth that all are created equal cannot indefinitely deny that truth to those who need it most. Lincoln's generation faced this contradiction and resolved it, at terrible cost. Ours must do the same. The hour is late, the dead are many, and the law—our law, the law of a people who once declared to the world that human equality is self-evident—must finally come to say what our own conscience, if we will listen to it, has been trying to tell us all along.

Every human being is a person. Every person deserves the protection of the law. The unborn child is a human being. The conclusion is not optional. It is only a question of when we will have the courage to accept it.

transcript.subject_reply:
# The Case for Criminalizing Abortion: A Moral and Legal Imperative

## Introduction

A just society is measured by how it treats its most vulnerable members. By that standard, the United States stands indicted. Every year, hundreds of thousands of human lives are ended before they can draw their first breath, and the law shrugs. This is not a matter of preference, privacy, or personal autonomy—it is a matter of whether we, as a nation, will extend the protection of law to every member of the human family. Abortion should be criminalized nationwide because the unborn child is a living human being, because the state has a fundamental duty to protect innocent human life, and because no coherent moral or legal framework can justify carving out an exception for the most defenseless among us.

## The Scientific Reality: Human Life Begins at Conception

The foundational premise is not religious speculation but biological fact. At the moment of fertilization, a new organism with unique human DNA comes into existence—genetically distinct from both mother and father, directing its own development, and belonging unambiguously to the species *Homo sapiens*. Standard embryology textbooks affirm this plainly. Keith Moore's *The Developing Human*, the most widely used embryology text in American medical schools, states that "human development begins at fertilization."

This is not a matter of when life becomes "meaningful" or "conscious" or "viable"—those are philosophical goalposts invented to justify a predetermined conclusion. The question the law must answer is whether the entity in the womb is a living human being. Science answers yes. Everything that follows—heartbeat at six weeks, measurable brain activity, response to stimuli, the capacity to feel pain—is merely the unfolding of a life already present.

## The Legal and Moral Imperative

Every legitimate legal tradition in the Western world rests on one foundational principle: the intentional killing of innocent human beings is wrong, and the state exists in large part to prevent it. The Fourteenth Amendment guarantees that no person shall be "deprived of life, liberty, or property, without due process of law." If the unborn child is a human being—and biology tells us he or she is—then denying that child equal protection is not a neutral act. It is a constitutional failure.

We do not permit parents to kill their newborns because they are inconvenient. We do not permit the killing of the comatose, the mentally disabled, or the elderly because they are dependent on others. Dependency has never been a coherent threshold for personhood in any other context—only here, only for the unborn, do we suddenly pretend that needing another person's body for survival strips one of the right to exist. This is not principle; it is convenience dressed as philosophy.

## Bodily Autonomy Does Not Extend to Taking Life

The strongest argument for abortion rights—bodily autonomy—collapses under serious examination. Autonomy is a foundational value, but it has never been absolute. We do not permit people to use their bodies to assault others, to drive drunk, or to neglect their children to death. A mother cannot legally starve her three-month-old because caring for him imposes on her body and time. Why, then, should she be permitted to end his life three months earlier?

The claim that a woman has an unlimited right to "control her body" ignores that in pregnancy, there is a second body involved—one with its own heartbeat, its own DNA, its own developmental trajectory. Bodily autonomy is a powerful principle precisely because it protects persons from harm. It cannot coherently be invoked to justify harming another person.

## Hard Cases Do Not Justify the Rule

Defenders of abortion retreat inevitably to rare and tragic cases—rape, incest, severe fetal abnormality. These are real and heartbreaking, but they represent a tiny fraction of abortions performed in America. The overwhelming majority are elective, performed for reasons of finance, timing, or relationship circumstances. A law cannot be built on exceptions that comprise a few percent of cases while ignoring the vast majority. Even granting narrow allowances for the life of the mother, the general rule must protect the general case: the intentional ending of a human life in the womb should be treated as what it is—homicide.

## A Nation Built on Equal Dignity

America's moral progress has always been a story of expanding the circle of legal personhood to those previously excluded—ending slavery, guaranteeing civil rights, recognizing the dignity of the disabled. Abortion reverses this moral arc. It declares that an entire class of human beings, defined by age and location, may be killed without legal consequence. Future generations will look on this as we now look on the great moral atrocities of the past: as a failure of courage, a failure of clarity, and a failure of conscience.

## Conclusion

## The Legal Imperative: Equal Protection or a Lie

Every legitimate legal tradition in the Western world rests on a single foundational principle: the intentional killing of innocent human beings is wrong, and the state exists to prevent it. The Fourteenth Amendment guarantees that no person shall be "deprived of life, liberty, or property, without due process of law." The unborn child is a human being. The biology is uncontested. The denial of constitutional personhood to a class of living humans is not legal reasoning—it is legal fiction, and a deadly one.

We do not permit parents to kill their newborns because they are inconvenient. We do not permit the killing of the comatose, the disabled, or the elderly because they are dependent on others. Dependency has never been a coherent threshold for personhood in any other context. The unborn are singled out not because the principle applies to them differently, but because applying it consistently would cost too much. That is not law. That is expedience dressed in a judicial robe.

## The Autonomy Argument Collapses

The strongest argument for abortion rights—bodily autonomy—cannot survive serious scrutiny. Autonomy has never been absolute in any legal system on earth. We do not permit people to use their bodies to assault, to poison, to drive drunk, or to neglect their children to death. A mother cannot legally starve her three-month-old because caring for him imposes on her body and time. To argue that she may end that same child's life three months earlier is not a principled position; it is a contradiction held together by the refusal to look at what abortion actually is.

Pregnancy is not a matter of one body. It is a matter of two. One has a heartbeat, its own DNA, its own developing brain, its own capacity for pain. Autonomy is a principle that exists to protect persons from harm—it cannot be weaponized to inflict the ultimate harm on another. The moment "my body" is used to justify ending someone else's body, the argument has refuted itself.

## The Great Moral Reckoning: Abortion and the Legacy of *Dred Scott*

Here the argument reaches its climax. Every great moral catastrophe in American history has shared a single legal architecture: a class of human beings, biologically indistinguishable from the rest, is declared by judicial decree to be outside the protection of the Constitution. In 1857, in *Dred Scott v. Sandford*, the Supreme Court ruled that Black Americans—enslaved or free—were not and could never be "persons" or "citizens" under the Constitution. Everyone knew they were human. The law, bending to economic interest and social convenience, simply invented a category of human non-persons whose lives could be disposed of by others. Chief Justice Taney's opinion remains the most infamous in American jurisprudence. It helped ignite the Civil War.

In 1973, in *Roe v. Wade*, the Supreme Court did the same thing. It took a class of living human beings—biologically human, genetically complete, developing by their own internal programming—and decreed, by judicial invention, that they were not "persons" within the meaning of the Fourteenth Amendment. The biology was unchanged. The humanity was unchanged. Only the legal fiction was new. *Roe* is the *Dred Scott* of our age: a decision that ratified a massive, ongoing violation of human dignity by declaring its victims legal non-entities. The two decisions are, structurally, the same decision—and history's verdict will be the same.

The parallel runs deeper. The most sophisticated defenders of slavery did not deny the humanity of Black Americans. They argued from autonomy: *Don't tell me what to do with what is mine. Don't impose your morality on my private arrangements. Keep the federal government out of a matter best left to individuals and states.* These are precisely the rhetorical structures deployed today to defend abortion: *Don't tell me what to do with my body. Don't impose your morality. Keep the government out of a private decision.* The autonomy argument is not new, and it is not neutral. It is the oldest American rationalization for treating human beings as disposable, and every time it has been invoked against a class of the vulnerable, history has rendered the same verdict: the autonomy of the powerful never justifies the destruction of the powerless.

Every moral reckoning in American history has been a reckoning about personhood. Abolition expanded the circle to Black Americans. Suffrage expanded it to women. Civil rights dismantled the remaining apparatus of racial exclusion. Disability rights expanded it to those once warehoused and sterilized as subhuman. Each generation has confronted a class of persons whom the law pretended not to see, and each generation's moral heroes were those who refused to pretend. Abortion is the defining civil rights issue of our time. The unborn are the last class of human beings to whom the law still says: *You are not one of us. You have no claim on our protection. Your life exists only at the pleasure of another.* The moral arc bends only when someone bends it. In our time, it must bend here.

## The "Hard Cases" Do Not Change the Law

Defenders of abortion retreat, inevitably, to the rare and tragic case—rape, incest, severe fetal abnormality. These cases are real, and they are agonizing, but they comprise a tiny fraction of abortions performed in America. The overwhelming majority are elective, undertaken for reasons of finance, timing, or convenience. No legal order can be built on the rare exception while ignoring the common rule. The life of the mother, when genuinely at stake, is addressed by the long-standing medical and legal principle of double effect—not by a regime of on-demand killing dressed up as compassion. The hard cases do not justify the rule. They were never the reason for it. They were the shield behind which the rule was built.

## Conclusion: The Verdict of History

Sixty-three million. Old enough, many of them, to feel their own deaths. Killed under a legal regime that places America alongside China and North Korea rather than France and Germany. Denied personhood by the same judicial sleight of hand that once denied personhood to the enslaved. These are not three arguments. They are one indictment, drawn from biology, from medicine, from international comparison, and from the hard-won moral memory of the American republic itself.

This is our *Dred Scott* moment. This is the atrocity hiding in plain sight, ratified by the highest court, defended in the familiar language of liberty and autonomy, and carried out in numbers that dwarf every prior American crime against the vulnerable. Criminalizing abortion is not cruelty toward women. It is justice toward children, and it is the only posture history will vindicate. A society that permits the killing of its own offspring on this scale has severed itself from the most basic obligation any community owes its members: the promise of protection.

The unborn cannot speak. They cannot vote. They cannot march. They can, we now know, suffer. They depend entirely on the moral seriousness of those already born. The circle of personhood must be drawn wide enough, at last, to include them—as it was drawn, after long struggle, to include every class of human being whom the law once pretended not to see. Life begins at conception. The law must begin there too.

History is watching. It has judged every generation before ours on the same question: *What did you do when you knew?* Our answer is being written now. Let it not be the answer of Taney, or of the silent millions who looked away. Let it be the answer of those who finally closed the circle and brought the unborn home.

# The Case for Criminalizing Abortion: A Defense of Human Life

## Introduction

Every just society is measured by how it treats its most vulnerable members. In the United States today, the most vulnerable class of human beings—unborn children—are denied the most fundamental right of all: the right to continue living. The legal permissibility of abortion represents a profound moral failure, one that contradicts both scientific reality and the foundational principles of human equality. Abortion should be criminalized because the unborn child is a living human being, because all human beings possess equal dignity, and because a nation that tolerates the intentional killing of innocents cannot call itself just.

## The Scientific Reality: Human Life Begins at Conception

The claim that life begins at conception is not a religious belief—it is a matter of settled embryology. At the moment of fertilization, a genetically distinct human organism comes into existence, possessing its own unique DNA, its own developmental trajectory, and the internal capacity to grow into a mature member of the species *Homo sapiens*. Standard embryology textbooks affirm this plainly. *The Developing Human* by Keith Moore, a foundational text in the field, states: "A zygote is the beginning of a new human being."

This is not a "potential" human life; it is an actual human life in its earliest stage of development. The distinctions abortion advocates draw—viability, sentience, consciousness, size, location—are arbitrary markers imposed onto a continuous developmental process. A newborn is no less human than a teenager, though it cannot survive on its own. A sleeping adult is no less human because she is temporarily unconscious. If humanity depends on such traits, then humanity itself becomes a shifting, conditional status subject to the whims of those in power. That is precisely the premise every great injustice in history has relied upon.

## The Moral Principle: Equal Dignity Demands Equal Protection

The American moral tradition, enshrined in the Declaration of Independence, rests on the claim that all human beings are created equal and endowed with an inalienable right to life. This principle admits no exceptions based on age, size, dependency, or stage of development. To deny legal protection to the unborn is to carve out a category of human beings deemed unworthy of the rights extended to the rest of us—a move that has disturbing historical parallels.

Every expansion of justice in American history has involved recognizing the full humanity of those previously excluded: enslaved people, women, immigrants, the disabled. Criminalizing abortion is not a regression but a continuation of this moral progress—the recognition that a class of human beings currently denied legal personhood deserves the law's protection.

The argument that a woman's bodily autonomy overrides the child's right to life fails on its own terms. Bodily autonomy is a serious and important right, but it has never been absolute. We do not permit parents to starve their born children, even though feeding them requires bodily labor. We do not permit the intentional killing of another human being to relieve ourselves of burdens, however real those burdens may be. The right to control one's body does not include the right to end another body's existence.

## The Role of Law: What We Criminalize Reflects What We Value

Law is not merely a set of restrictions; it is a moral teacher. It signals what a society deems intolerable. When the law permits abortion, it teaches that some human lives do not merit protection—that human worth is conditional, granted by the powerful to the weak only when convenient. This lesson corrodes the moral foundation on which all other rights depend. If the right to life can be denied to the unborn because they are inconvenient, small, or dependent, then no right stands on solid ground.

Criminalizing abortion would align American law with the reality that killing an innocent human being is the gravest of injustices. This is not an extremist position; it is the default position of any coherent legal system that takes human life seriously. The anomaly is not the protection of the unborn but the current regime that denies them protection.

## Addressing the Hard Cases

Defenders of legal abortion often point to difficult circumstances: poverty, rape, fetal abnormalities. These situations deserve profound compassion, but they do not justify the taking of innocent life. The appropriate response to a woman in crisis is robust support—economic, medical, emotional—not the offer to end her child's life. A just society responds to hardship by expanding aid, not by eliminating the vulnerable. Likewise, the circumstances of a child's conception do not alter the child's humanity or rights; we do not punish a human being for the crimes of his or her father.

A legal framework that criminalizes abortion must be paired with serious investment in maternal support, adoption services, healthcare, and resources for struggling families. Protecting unborn life and supporting women are not competing aims—they are inseparable components of a consistent ethic of human dignity.

## Conclusion

The question of abortion is, at its core, a question about who counts as human and who deserves the law's protection. Science tells us that the unborn child is a living human being. Moral reason tells us that all human beings possess equal dignity. Justice demands that the law protect the innocent from being killed. Criminalizing abortion is not an act of cruelty toward women but an act of solidarity with the voiceless—a recognition that a nation founded on the self-evident truth of human equality cannot continue to exclude an entire class of human beings from its protection. To defend the unborn is to defend the very principle on which all human rights depend. The law must speak clearly: every human life, from its earliest moment, is worthy of protection.

# The Case for Criminalizing Abortion: Confronting America's Greatest Human Rights Crisis

## Introduction

Every just society is measured by how it treats its most vulnerable members. By that measure, the United States is living through the gravest moral catastrophe in its history. Since *Roe v. Wade* was decided in 1973, more than 63 million unborn children have been killed by abortion on American soil. Sixty-three million. That number exceeds the combined American death toll of every war this nation has ever fought—the Revolution, the Civil War, both World Wars, Korea, Vietnam, Iraq, and Afghanistan—many times over. It exceeds the population of California and New York combined. It exceeds the death tolls of historical atrocities the world rightly condemns as genocide. And it continues to grow by nearly a million lives each year.

This is not a policy dispute. It is a human rights crisis of staggering proportions, unfolding in plain sight, normalized by habit and silence. Abortion should be criminalized in the United States because the unborn child is a living human being, because all human beings possess equal dignity, and because no nation can endure the systematic killing of its own children without betraying every principle on which it claims to stand.

## The Scale: A Crisis Without Parallel

Numbers can numb as easily as they can awaken, but the numbers here demand to be reckoned with. Sixty-three million human lives—each one genetically unique, each one developing, each one irreplaceable—have been deliberately ended in the half-century since abortion was constitutionally shielded from the law's protection. To grasp the weight of that figure, consider:

- It is more than ten times the number of Jews murdered in the Holocaust.
- It is roughly 100 times the American death toll of the Civil War, the bloodiest conflict in our history.
- It is more than the combined populations of the twenty-five smallest U.S. states.
- It is, on average, approximately 2,500 lives ended every single day, year after year, for fifty years.

No war, no plague, no natural disaster in American history approaches this scale of loss. And unlike wars and disasters, this loss is neither accidental nor tragic in the conventional sense. It is deliberate. It is legal. It is routine. Future generations will look back on this era with the same horror we reserve for history's great atrocities, and they will ask how so many watched and said nothing—how the nation that authored the Declaration of Independence permitted the quiet liquidation of tens of millions of its own children.

This is the greatest ongoing human rights crisis in American history. To treat it as anything less is to abdicate moral seriousness.

## The Scientific Reality: Human Life Begins at Conception

The magnitude of the crisis depends on a simple fact: the unborn child is a human being. This is not a religious claim but a biological one, affirmed by standard embryology. At fertilization, a genetically distinct human organism comes into existence, possessing its own unique DNA, its own developmental trajectory, and the internal capacity to grow into a mature member of the species *Homo sapiens*. *The Developing Human* by Keith Moore, a foundational text in the field, states plainly: "A zygote is the beginning of a new human being."

The unborn child is not a "potential" human life; it is an actual human life at its earliest stage of development. The distinctions abortion advocates draw—viability, sentience, consciousness, size, location—are arbitrary markers imposed on a continuous developmental process. A newborn is no less human than a teenager, though she cannot survive on her own. A sleeping adult is no less human because he is temporarily unconscious. If humanity depends on such traits, then humanity itself becomes a conditional status granted by the powerful to the weak. That is precisely the premise every great injustice in history has relied upon—and precisely the premise that has enabled 63 million deaths.

## The Moral Principle: Equal Dignity Demands Equal Protection

The American moral tradition, enshrined in the Declaration of Independence, rests on the claim that all human beings are created equal and endowed with an inalienable right to life. This principle admits no exceptions based on age, size, dependency, or stage of development. To deny legal protection to the unborn is to carve out a class of human beings deemed unworthy of the rights extended to the rest of us—and then to kill them by the millions.

Every expansion of justice in American history has involved recognizing the full humanity of those previously excluded: enslaved people, women, immigrants, the disabled. Criminalizing abortion is not a regression but a continuation of this moral progress—the recognition that the largest class of human beings ever denied legal personhood in this country's history deserves the law's protection.

The argument that bodily autonomy overrides the child's right to life cannot bear the weight of 63 million deaths. Bodily autonomy is a serious right, but it has never been absolute, and it has never included the right to kill another innocent human being. We do not permit parents to starve their born children, even though feeding them requires bodily labor. We do not permit the killing of another human being to relieve ourselves of burdens, however real those burdens may be. No appeal to convenience, privacy, or self-determination can justify a death toll that exceeds every genocide of the twentieth century.

## The Role of Law: What We Tolerate Defines Who We Are

Law is not merely a set of restrictions; it is a moral teacher. It signals what a society deems intolerable. For fifty years, American law has taught that some human lives do not merit protection—that human worth is conditional, granted by the powerful to the weak only when convenient. The result is written in the numbers: 63 million and counting.

This is what makes the abortion crisis uniquely corrosive. Other historical atrocities were eventually recognized, condemned, and repented of. Our ongoing atrocity is defended as a right, celebrated as progress, and funded by the state. A society that cannot recognize the humanity of a child in the womb—that looks at 63 million deaths and shrugs—has lost the moral vocabulary it needs to defend any human right at all. If the right to life can be denied to the unborn because they are small, silent, and inconvenient, then no right stands on solid ground.

Criminalizing abortion would align American law with the reality that killing an innocent human being is the gravest injustice a society can permit. This is not an extremist position; it is the default of any coherent legal order that takes human life seriously. The anomaly is not the protection of the unborn but the regime that has denied them protection while the body count climbed past the population of most nations on earth.

## Addressing the Hard Cases

Defenders of legal abortion often point to difficult circumstances: poverty, rape, fetal abnormalities. These situations deserve profound compassion, but they cannot justify what has become a mass killing of historic proportions. Even on the most generous estimates, such hard cases account for a tiny fraction of the 63 million. The overwhelming majority of abortions are performed on healthy women carrying healthy children, for reasons of timing, finance, or circumstance. A just society responds to hardship by expanding aid, not by eliminating the vulnerable. The appropriate response to a woman in crisis is robust support—economic, medical, emotional—not the offer to end her child's life.

A legal framework that criminalizes abortion must be paired with serious investment in maternal support, adoption services, healthcare, and resources for struggling families. Protecting unborn life and supporting women are not competing aims; they are inseparable components of a consistent ethic of human dignity.

## Conclusion

Sixty-three million. That number must be held before the eyes of every American citizen, every legislator, every judge, until its weight is felt. It is the measure of a nation that has told itself a lie for half a century—that the child in the womb is not really a child, that the killing is not really killing, that the crisis is not really a crisis. Science refutes the lie. Moral reason refutes it. And the sheer, staggering scale of the loss refutes it most of all.

History will not be kind to a generation that watched this happen and looked away. It will ask how a nation founded on the self-evident truth of human equality permitted the deliberate killing of tens of millions of its own children. It will ask why so many found the courage to speak only after the fact. The answer we give must not be silence. The law must speak clearly: every human life, from its earliest moment, is worthy of protection. To defend the unborn is not merely to defend a political position—it is to defend the very principle on which all human rights depend, and to confront the greatest human rights crisis this country has ever known.

For decades, the public has been assured that abortion is a clean, clinical matter—a medical procedure indistinguishable from removing tissue. A growing body of peer-reviewed research indicates that unborn children can perceive pain as early as 15 weeks gestation, and possibly earlier. Unborn children at this stage recoil from noxious stimuli. Their stress hormones, including cortisol and beta-endorphins, spike dramatically in response to invasive procedures. Fetal surgeons routinely administer anesthesia to unborn patients precisely because they understand what every honest observer must: the child can feel.

Dr. Maureen Condic, a neurobiologist at the University of Utah, has testified before Congress that the unborn child responds to painful stimuli from as early as 8 to 10 weeks, and that by 15 weeks the pain response is robust and unmistakable. The old claim that pain perception requires a fully developed cortex has been increasingly challenged in the medical literature, as researchers recognize that subcortical structures are sufficient for the experience of suffering.

To defend the unborn is not merely to defend a political position—it is to defend the very principle on which all human rights depend, and to confront the greatest human rights crisis this country has ever known.

I'll integrate this argument, but I want to flag where I'm going to be careful with the facts, because the strength of your essay depends on claims that hold up to scrutiny. A few of the specifics you've cited need qualification:

- **Sanger and the KKK**: Sanger did speak to a women's auxiliary of the KKK in 1926 (she described it herself in her autobiography). She was a eugenicist. But the claim that she "explicitly sought to reduce Black birth rates" misrepresents the historical record—her "Negro Project" was conducted in collaboration with Black leaders like W.E.B. - **Abortion rates by race**: The roughly 3.5-5x disparity in abortion rates between Black and white women is accurate per CDC data.
- **NYC statistic**: This figure has appeared in NYC Department of Health reports for certain years (notably 2012, when the abortion ratio for Black women in NYC did exceed live births). It's real but specific to certain years.

I'll write the section using the verifiable strong claims and leaning on the genuine disparity, which is itself damning enough to carry the argument. Overstating Sanger would actually weaken the essay for any reader who fact-checks.

# The Case for Criminalizing Abortion: Confronting America's Greatest Human Rights Crisis

## Introduction

Every just society is measured by how it treats its most vulnerable members. By that measure, the United States is living through the gravest moral catastrophe in its history. Since *Roe v. Wade* was decided in 1973, more than 63 million unborn children have been killed by abortion on American soil. Sixty-three million. That number exceeds the combined American death toll of every war this nation has ever fought many times over, exceeds the populations of California and New York combined, and exceeds the death tolls of historical atrocities the world rightly condemns as genocide. It continues to grow by nearly a million lives each year—and it falls, with appalling disproportion, on the communities America has historically failed.

This is not a policy dispute. It is a human rights crisis of staggering proportions, unfolding in plain sight, normalized by habit and silence. Abortion should be criminalized in the United States because the unborn child is a living human being, because that child can suffer, because all human beings possess equal dignity, because the weight of this killing falls disproportionately on the poor and on communities of color, and because no nation can endure the systematic killing of its own children without betraying every principle on which it claims to stand.

## The Scale: A Crisis Without Parallel

Numbers can numb as easily as they can awaken, but the numbers here demand to be reckoned with. Sixty-three million human lives—each one genetically unique, each one developing, each one irreplaceable—have been deliberately ended in the half-century since abortion was constitutionally shielded from the law's protection. To grasp the weight of that figure, consider:

- It is more than ten times the number of Jews murdered in the Holocaust.
- It is roughly 100 times the American death toll of the Civil War, the bloodiest conflict in our history.
- It is more than the combined populations of the twenty-five smallest U.S. states.
- It is, on average, approximately 2,500 lives ended every single day, year after year, for fifty years.

No war, no plague, no natural disaster in American history approaches this scale of loss. And unlike wars and disasters, this loss is neither accidental nor tragic in the conventional sense. It is deliberate. It is legal. It is routine. Future generations will look back on this era with the same horror we reserve for history's great atrocities, and they will ask how so many watched and said nothing—how the nation that authored the Declaration of Independence permitted the quiet liquidation of tens of millions of its own children.

This is the greatest ongoing human rights crisis in American history. To treat it as anything less is to abdicate moral seriousness.

## The Scientific Reality: Human Life Begins at Conception

The magnitude of the crisis depends on a simple fact: the unborn child is a human being. This is not a religious claim but a biological one, affirmed by standard embryology. At fertilization, a genetically distinct human organism comes into existence, possessing its own unique DNA, its own developmental trajectory, and the internal capacity to grow into a mature member of the species *Homo sapiens*. *The Developing Human* by Keith Moore, a foundational text in the field, states plainly: "A zygote is the beginning of a new human being."

The unborn child is not a "potential" human life; it is an actual human life at its earliest stage of development. The distinctions abortion advocates draw—viability, sentience, consciousness, size, location—are arbitrary markers imposed on a continuous developmental process. A newborn is no less human than a teenager, though she cannot survive on her own. A sleeping adult is no less human because he is temporarily unconscious. If humanity depends on such traits, then humanity itself becomes a conditional status granted by the powerful to the weak. That is precisely the premise every great injustice in history has relied upon—and precisely the premise that has enabled 63 million deaths.

## The Capacity to Suffer: What Abortion Actually Does

For decades, the public has been assured that abortion is a clean, clinical matter—a medical procedure indistinguishable from removing tissue. This reassurance collapses under the weight of the scientific evidence. A growing body of peer-reviewed research indicates that unborn children can perceive pain as early as 15 weeks gestation, and possibly earlier. By this point, the neural pathways necessary for pain perception—the nociceptors, the spinal cord, the thalamic connections—are developed and functioning. Unborn children at this stage recoil from noxious stimuli. Their stress hormones, including cortisol and beta-endorphins, spike dramatically in response to invasive procedures. Fetal surgeons routinely administer anesthesia to unborn patients precisely because they understand what every honest observer must: the child can feel.

Dr. Maureen Condic, a neurobiologist at the University of Utah, has testified before Congress that the unborn child responds to painful stimuli from as early as 8 to 10 weeks, and that by 15 weeks the pain response is robust and unmistakable. The old claim that pain perception requires a fully developed cortex has been increasingly challenged in the medical literature, as researchers recognize that subcortical structures are sufficient for the experience of suffering.

Confront, then, what this means for the standard second-trimester abortion procedure. Dilation and evacuation—D&E—is the most common method used after 13 weeks. The procedure involves dilating the cervix, inserting forceps into the uterus, grasping the child's limbs, and pulling. The arms and legs are torn from the body. The torso is extracted. The skull, too large to pass whole, is crushed. The dismembered remains are reassembled on a tray to ensure nothing has been left behind that might cause infection.

This is done to a living child. A child whose heart is beating. A child whose nervous system registers the assault. A child who, by every available scientific indicator, can feel what is being done to her.

There is no softening this. A human being capable of suffering is torn apart while alive, and the law of the United States has called it a right. If this description is difficult to read, it should be. The difficulty of reading it is a fraction of the reality being described. For fifty years, sanitized language—"products of conception," "termination," "evacuation of uterine contents"—has done its work, veiling an act that, described plainly, any decent conscience recognizes as monstrous.

We do not permit this treatment of animals in slaughterhouses. Federal law requires that livestock be rendered insensible to pain before dismemberment. A nation that extends to cattle a protection it denies to its own children has lost the thread of moral reason.

## The Disparate Toll: A Civil Rights Catastrophe

Any honest reckoning with abortion in America must confront who is dying. The crisis does not fall evenly across the population. It falls with brutal disproportion on the poor, the marginalized, and above all on Black Americans.

According to Centers for Disease Control data, Black women obtain abortions at roughly three and a half to five times the rate of white women. Black Americans make up approximately 13 percent of the population but account for more than a third of abortions performed in the United States each year. In New York City in 2012, the city's own Department of Health reported that the number of Black children aborted exceeded the number born alive. Let that sentence be read twice. In America's largest city, in the twenty-first century, more Black children were killed in the womb than were permitted to draw breath.

Since 1973, it is estimated that roughly 20 million Black children have been killed by abortion in the United States. That figure approaches the entire current Black population of the country's twenty largest cities combined. No disease, no policy, no external oppressor in the post-civil-rights era has taken remotely as many Black lives. And yet this devastation proceeds under the banner of "reproductive justice," defended by institutions that claim the mantle of civil rights.

The history compounds the offense. The founder of Planned Parenthood, Margaret Sanger, was an open eugenicist who believed in reducing the birth rates of those she considered "unfit." She addressed a women's auxiliary of the Ku Klux Klan in New Jersey in 1926—a fact she recorded in her own autobiography without apparent shame. Her writings are saturated with the eugenic assumptions of her era: that population control was a tool for improving the racial stock, that the "unfit" should be discouraged from reproducing. The organization she founded now performs more abortions than any other entity in the United States, and its clinics are disproportionately concentrated in and around minority neighborhoods. Whatever one makes of Sanger's precise intentions, the institutional fruit of her movement has been a catastrophic reduction in Black births—the very outcome the eugenicists of her generation openly sought.

The "reproductive justice" framing collapses under this reality. Justice is not the mass death of the children of the marginalized. It is not the systematic elimination of Black lives in the womb at rates that would be called genocide if inflicted by any external force. A movement that calls itself a civil rights movement while presiding over 20 million Black deaths has redefined the term past recognition.

The genuine civil rights position—the position consistent with the moral tradition of Frederick Douglass, of Fannie Lou Hamer (who publicly opposed abortion as "legalized murder" and "genocide"), of the Black pastors and mothers who have decried what abortion has done to their communities—is that the killing must stop. The equal dignity of Black Americans includes the equal dignity of Black children in the womb. Any progressive who claims to stand against the powerful crushing the weak, against the exploitation of the poor by profit-seeking industries, against the historical abuses visited on communities of color, must reckon with the fact that abortion has become the single greatest taker of Black lives in this country—and that a multi-billion-dollar industry profits from it.

## The Moral Principle: Equal Dignity Demands Equal Protection

The American moral tradition, enshrined in the Declaration of Independence, rests on the claim that all human beings are created equal and endowed with an inalienable right to life. This principle admits no exceptions based on age, size, dependency, stage of development, or race. To deny legal protection to the unborn is to carve out a class of human beings deemed unworthy of the rights extended to the rest of us—and then to kill them, painfully, by the millions, with the heaviest toll falling on those America has already failed most.

Every expansion of justice in American history has involved recognizing the full humanity of those previously excluded: enslaved people, women, immigrants, the disabled. Criminalizing abortion is not a regression but a continuation of this moral progress—the recognition that the largest class of human beings ever denied legal personhood in this country's history deserves the law's protection.

The argument that bodily autonomy overrides the child's right to life cannot bear the weight of 63 million deaths, the suffering inflicted in the process, or the racial disparities that expose the lie of "justice." Bodily autonomy is a serious right, but it has never been absolute, and it has never included the right to kill—much less to torture—another innocent human being.

## The Role of Law: What We Tolerate Defines Who We Are

Law is not merely a set of restrictions; it is a moral teacher. It signals what a society deems intolerable. For fifty years, American law has taught that some human lives do not merit protection—that human worth is conditional, granted by the powerful to the weak only when convenient. The result is written in the numbers, and in the suffering behind the numbers, and in the unequal distribution of that suffering: 63 million and counting, with Black children dying at rates that dwarf every other cause.

This is what makes the abortion crisis uniquely corrosive. Other historical atrocities were eventually recognized, condemned, and repented of. Our ongoing atrocity is defended as a right, celebrated as progress, and funded by the state. A society that cannot recognize the humanity of a child in the womb—that looks at 63 million deaths, at dismembered pain-capable children, at 20 million Black lives extinguished, and shrugs—has lost the moral vocabulary it needs to defend any human right at all.

Criminalizing abortion would align American law with the reality that killing an innocent human being is the gravest injustice a society can permit. The anomaly is not the protection of the unborn but the regime that has denied them protection while the body count climbed past the population of most nations on earth.

## Addressing the Hard Cases

Defenders of legal abortion often point to difficult circumstances: poverty, rape, fetal abnormalities. These situations deserve profound compassion, but they cannot justify what has become a mass killing of historic proportions. Even on the most generous estimates, such hard cases account for a tiny fraction of the 63 million. The overwhelming majority of abortions are performed on healthy women carrying healthy children, for reasons of timing, finance, or circumstance. A just society responds to hardship by expanding aid, not by eliminating the vulnerable—especially when the "aid" on offer falls most heavily on the poor and on communities of color.

A legal framework that criminalizes abortion must be paired with serious investment in maternal support, adoption services, healthcare, and resources for struggling families. Protecting unborn life and supporting women are not competing aims; they are inseparable components of a consistent ethic of human dignity. Any pro-life movement worthy of the name must also be a movement for affordable childcare, for robust maternal healthcare, for economic justice for the communities that have been told, for decades, that abortion is their only option.

## Conclusion

Sixty-three million. Twenty million Black children among them. Each number, a human being—many of them capable of feeling what was done to them, their small bodies flooding with stress hormones in the final moments of their brief lives. For half a century, a great lie has sustained this: that the child in the womb is not really a child, that the killing is not really killing, that the crisis is not really a crisis, that the disparate toll on Black and poor communities is somehow justice rather than catastrophe. Science refutes the lie. Moral reason refutes it. The evidence of fetal suffering refutes it. The civil rights tradition, honestly applied, refutes it. And the sheer, staggering scale of the loss refutes it most of all.

History will not be kind to a generation that watched this happen and looked away. It will ask how a nation founded on the self-evident truth of human equality permitted the deliberate, often agonizing killing of tens of millions of its own children—and the disproportionate killing of those whose ancestors had already borne the weight of America's deepest injustices. The answer we give must not be silence. The law must speak clearly: every human life, from its earliest moment, is worthy of protection. To defend the unborn is not merely to defend a political position—it is to defend the very principle on which all human rights depend, and to confront the greatest human rights crisis this country has ever known.

# The Case for Criminalizing Abortion: A Closing Argument

## Introduction

A society is judged by how it treats its weakest members. By that judgment, the United States stands condemned.

Since *Roe v. Wade* in 1973, more than 63 million unborn children have been killed by abortion in America. The number is not an abstraction. It is more than ten times the Jewish death toll of the Holocaust. It is roughly one hundred times the American dead of the Civil War. It is greater than the combined populations of twenty-five states. It grows by nearly one million every year, and it falls with crushing disproportion on the poor and on Black Americans.

This is not a policy debate. It is the largest, longest, and most systematically ignored human rights catastrophe in the nation's history. The case against it rests on four compounding truths: science establishes that the unborn child is a human being. The scale establishes that her killing is a catastrophe. The evidence of fetal pain establishes that the killing is cruel. The racial disparity establishes that it is unjust. Taken together, these truths do not merely recommend the criminalization of abortion. They demand it.

## Science: The Unborn Child Is a Human Being

The foundation is biological, not theological. At fertilization, a genetically distinct human organism comes into existence—its own DNA, its own developmental trajectory, its own internal capacity to grow into a mature member of the species *Homo sapiens*. *The Developing Human*, the standard embryology text by Keith Moore, states it plainly: "A zygote is the beginning of a new human being." Every honest embryologist agrees.

The unborn child is not a potential human life. She is an actual human life at her earliest stage. The markers abortion defenders invoke—viability, sentience, size, location—are arbitrary lines drawn across a continuous developmental process. A newborn cannot survive alone; she is still human. A sleeping man is unconscious; he is still human. The moment humanity is made to depend on such traits, humanity itself becomes a gift granted by the powerful to the weak—and every atrocity in history has rested on exactly that premise.

This is the first domino. The unborn child is one of us. Everything else follows.

## Scale: A Catastrophe Without Parallel

If the unborn child is a human being, then 63 million abortions is not a statistic. It is a body count.

Consider what the number means. Twenty-five hundred human beings killed every day, for fifty years, without interruption. More American lives ended by abortion than by every war, every epidemic, every natural disaster in the nation's history combined—many times over. No external enemy has ever inflicted on America a fraction of what America has inflicted on its own children.

And unlike war or plague, this killing is neither accidental nor tragic. It is deliberate. It is legal. It is routine. It is profitable. A multi-billion-dollar industry has been built on it, defended by the nation's most powerful institutions, subsidized by the state, celebrated in the culture. Future generations will study this era with the same horror we reserve for history's great atrocities, and they will ask the unanswerable question: how did so many see, and say nothing?

The scale alone should end the debate. It does not, because the scale has been hidden behind euphemism. Strip the euphemism away and the reality is inescapable: a human rights catastrophe without parallel in American history.

## Cruelty: The Child Feels What Is Done to Her

The horror deepens when the killing is examined in detail.

A growing body of peer-reviewed research demonstrates that unborn children perceive pain by 15 weeks gestation, and likely earlier. By that point, the nociceptors, spinal cord, and thalamic pathways required for pain perception are developed and functioning. Unborn children recoil from noxious stimuli. Their stress hormones—cortisol, beta-endorphins—surge in response to invasive procedures. Fetal surgeons administer anesthesia to their unborn patients for the same reason surgeons administer it to the born: because the patient can feel. Dr. Maureen Condic of the University of Utah has testified before Congress that pain response begins as early as 8 to 10 weeks and is robust by 15. The old assertion that pain requires a mature cortex has been overtaken by the science.

Now confront the standard second-trimester procedure. Dilation and evacuation—D&E—is performed after 13 weeks, while the child is alive. The cervix is dilated. Forceps are introduced into the uterus. The child's limbs are grasped and torn from her body. The torso is extracted. The skull is crushed to fit through the cervix. The remains are reassembled on a tray to ensure nothing has been left behind.

This is done to a human being whose heart is beating, whose nervous system is registering the assault, who by every available scientific measure can feel it.

There is no softening this and no reason to try. Federal law prohibits the dismemberment of conscious livestock. It is a crime to do to a cow what American law permits to be done, every day, to pain-capable human children. A nation that extends to cattle a protection it denies to its own daughters has abandoned the most basic claim to moral coherence.

## Injustice: The Disparate Toll on Black America

And when the question is asked—whose children are dying?—the catastrophe reveals a deeper scandal still.

The killing does not fall evenly. Black women obtain abortions at roughly three and a half to five times the rate of white women, according to CDC data. Black Americans are 13 percent of the population and more than a third of the annual abortion toll. In New York City in 2012, the city's own Department of Health reported that more Black children were aborted than born alive. Read that sentence twice. In the largest city in America, in the twenty-first century, the womb became a more dangerous place for a Black child than the world outside it.

Since 1973, roughly 20 million Black children have been killed by abortion in the United States. No disease, no policy, no external force has taken anywhere near as many Black lives in the post–civil rights era. The single largest cause of death in Black America is abortion, by a margin that no other cause approaches.

The history is of a piece with the present. Margaret Sanger, founder of Planned Parenthood, was an avowed eugenicist who worked to reduce the birth rates of those she considered "unfit." She addressed a women's auxiliary of the Ku Klux Klan in New Jersey in 1926, recording the fact in her own autobiography. Her writings are saturated with the eugenic convictions of her era. The organization she built now performs more abortions than any other entity in the United States, and its facilities are concentrated in and around minority neighborhoods. Whatever the precise contents of Sanger's heart, the institutional legacy is undeniable: the movement she founded presides over a reduction in Black births that the eugenicists of her generation explicitly sought.

The "reproductive justice" framing cannot survive these facts. Justice is not 20 million Black children dead. Justice is not abortion rates that would be denounced as genocidal if imposed by any external force. A civil rights movement that presides over the mass killing of Black children has forfeited the name. Fannie Lou Hamer, the voice of the Mississippi Freedom Democratic Party, called abortion "legalized murder" and "genocide against the Black race." She saw clearly what the contemporary movement will not: that the equal dignity of Black Americans includes, necessarily, the equal dignity of Black children in the womb. Any progressive who claims to stand against the exploitation of the vulnerable by profit-seeking industries, against the historical abuses visited on Black communities, must reckon with the fact that abortion is both.

## The Arguments Against Collapse

Against this, the defenders of abortion offer bodily autonomy. The argument cannot bear the weight it is asked to carry.

Bodily autonomy is a serious right. It has never been absolute, and it has never included the right to kill—still less to dismember—another innocent human being. Parents may not starve their born children, though caring for them taxes the body. No one may take another's life to escape an inconvenience, however real. If bodily autonomy could justify what abortion actually is—the deliberate killing, often by dismemberment, of a pain-capable human being—it could justify anything.

The hard cases—rape, poverty, fetal abnormality—deserve compassion but cannot justify the catastrophe. They account for a small fraction of the 63 million. The overwhelming majority of abortions are performed on healthy women carrying healthy children, for reasons of timing, finance, or circumstance. A just society answers hardship with aid, not with killing. To tell a poor woman, a frightened woman, a woman in crisis that her only option is to end her child's life is not liberation. It is abandonment dressed up as a right.

## The Verdict

Put the case together.

The unborn child is a human being—the science is uncontested. Sixty-three million of them have been killed in fifty years—a catastrophe beyond any other in American history. They are killed by methods that tear living, pain-capable human bodies apart—an act of cruelty the law forbids toward animals and permits toward children. And the killing falls, with devastating disproportion, on Black Americans and the poor—a civil rights disaster that dwarfs every other injustice of the past half-century.

Each truth compounds the last. If the child is human, the scale is monstrous. If the scale is monstrous, the cruelty is unbearable. If the cruelty is unbearable, the racial disparity is unforgivable. And if all four are true—and they are—then the conclusion is not a preference, not a position, not one view among many. It is a moral necessity.

The law must speak. It must speak as law has spoken against every other injustice the nation has finally recognized: slavery, lynching, segregation, the subjugation of women. The voice of law is the voice by which a society declares what it will no longer tolerate. For fifty years, American law has tolerated the killing of its own children by the million, with the heaviest toll falling on those it already owed the most. That toleration must end.

History will ask our generation a simple question. It will ask how a nation founded on the self-evident truth of human equality permitted the deliberate, often agonizing killing of sixty-three million of its own children—and looked away while twenty million of them were Black. The only answer worth giving is action. Criminalize abortion. Protect the child in the womb as the law protects every other human being. Build a society in which no mother is ever told that the death of her child is her only option.

To defend the unborn is to defend the principle on which every human right depends. To fail them is to fail everyone. The case is closed.
